Lake effect snows developed on Thursday (20th) as a west to northwest flow set up off Lake Erie and brought narrow streamers of heavy snow into Chautauqua County. Several inches fell during the afternoon. Meanwhile, a weak secondary front and wind shift dropped south across New York and set up from near Erie across Pennsylvania to near Philadelphia. A band of heavy snow set up along this convergence line, fed at its western end by Lake Erie. This band dropped just to the south of Chautauqua County during the evening, after 6 to 8 inch totals. Winds turned north-northeasterly and another band set up from Dunkirk to Ripley which became heavy overnight and early Friday morning as winds backed to north-northwest. Six to twelve inches fell during this phase along the lakeshore. The flow turned strong northwest later Friday and remained so through Saturday. This flow was unusually cold for November but very dry, so multiple bands formed off Lake Ontario and continued for 36 to 48 hours. Total snowfall for the entire event exceeded a foot over most of Chautauqua County. Eight to fourteen inches fell for the period from Wayne to Oswego counties. Specific snowfall reports included: 18��� at Mayville, 16��� at Westfield, 14��� at Jamestown and West Monroe; 12 inches at Cherry Hill; 11��� at Dunkirk; 10��� at Ripley, Oswego and Palymra; and 8��� at Cato.

A powerful storm moved from the Mississippi River Valley on December 24th across the Great Lakes and into eastern Quebec by the morning of December 25th. A strong cold front associated with this storm moved across northern New York during the late evening. South to southwest winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 40 mph ahead of the cold front shifted to the west-northwest at 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph during early Christmas morning. Scattered reports of tree limbs, power outages and torn roof shingles were reported.

A surface low and associated arctic cold front moved across northern New York and New England on the night of December 7th. This delivered the season's first arctic air mass with temperatures of zero to 15 below zero by the morning of December 8th along with brisk northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph with higher gusts at times. Wind chill readings during the early to mid-morning hours of December 8th were 20 to 30 below zero across northern New York.

An area of high pressure was located across New England and New York on the morning of December 11th.  Meanwhile, complex low pressure that originated in the Mississippi River, traveled north along the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the morning of December 11th.  This low quickly raced up the coast and was located over Long Island by the morning of December 12th and downeast Maine by the afternoon. This system tapped into mild, moist Atlantic air which then traveled above the colder air mass entrenched across the region.||Snow began across the Champlain Valley of New York during the evening of the 11th and briefly mixed with sleet, before precipitation ended as snow by late morning of the 12th. Snowfall accumulations in the Champlain Valley of New York were 4 to 8 inches with some sleet in Essex county.

A cold westerly wind flow developed behind a departing low pressure system. These westerly winds brought a band of lake effect snow across the southern Tug Hill Plateau region east of Lake Ontario. The snow fell from the evening on the 16th through the morning on the 17th before weakening and moving south of the region. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 8 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent lake effect snow brought 8 to 16 inches of snow to northern Oneida county.

Temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s across much of the mid Hudson Valley and Capital Region during each afternoon. The combination of high temperatures and humidity levels produced heat indices of 100 to 104 degrees.||Many schools across the region either cancelled classes, or had early dismissals due to the extreme heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper level closed low and associated pool of cold air aloft dropped south through the lower Great Lakes. Thunderstorms developed across the eastern Finger Lakes and eastern Lake Ontario Region during the afternoon hours. Hail, up to an inch-and-a-quarter diameter, fell in Cayuga, Ontario and Oswego counties. Just outside of Victor, Ontario County, the large hail shattered automobile windshields. Lightning from the thunderstorms struck the Bristol Springs Free Church bell tower.

A potent mid-level shortwave dropped southeast from Lake Huron during the pre-dawn hours initiating thunderstorms across Lake Erie and the western Southern Tier of New York. The line of storms produced damaging winds across Chautauqua County and then produced up to one inch hail over parts of Cattaraugus County. Trees and power lines were downed with scattered power outages reported in Mayville, Dewittville, Ellery Center and Gerry. Utilities reported several thousand without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid-level shortwave dropped southeast from Lake Huron during the pre-dawn hours initiating thunderstorms across Lake Erie and the western Southern Tier of New York. Trees and power lines were downed with scattered power outages reported in Mayville, Dewittville, Ellery Center and Gerry. Utilities reported several thousand without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in an unstable airmass ahead of an approaching cold front. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds the downed power lines in Niagara Falls.  The strong thunderstorms dropped several inches of rain across parts of Wyoming County that resulted in flash flooding from Gainesville to Silver Springs.

Thunderstorms crossed Lake Erie during the early morning hours. An isolated storm the moved onshore over southern Erie county produced damaging winds estimated to 60 mph. The winds overturned three boats stored on shore and downed trees and large limbs.

Thunderstorms that developed in an unstable air mass during the afternoon hours were enhanced by lake breezes. A thunderstorm over Chautauqua County produced downburst winds estimated near 60 mph that downed trees and power lines in Findley Lake. The heavy rain that accompanied the storm resulted in flash flooding. Route 426 was closed due to flooding.  Elsewhere, thunderstorms produced hail up to one inch in Niagara Falls, Niagara County and just outside of North Osceola, Lewis County.

A strong cold front swept east across the region during the evening hours of Saturday October 25th. A wave of low pressure developed along this front and lifted north during the evening, creating locally heavy rainfall across portions of eastern New York and western New England.||In addition, the tight pressure gradient between the approaching front, and strong high pressure off the New England coast, created gusty southeast to south winds across the region from Saturday afternoon into the evening, which led to some downed trees, tree limbs and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down near East Fishkill due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a moderately unstable airmass across the Champlain Valley of New York during the afternoon of June 16th. Scattered thunderstorms developed along this cold front with quarter size diameter hail reported in Crown Point (Essex County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail reported in Crown Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong cold frontal passage, and the extratropical remnants of Ike led to strong winds across the southern and western Adirondacks, the western and central Mohawk Valley, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and Schoharie Valley of eastern New York during the early morning hours of Monday September 15th. Measured wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were recorded, leading to numerous downed trees, tree limbs and power lines across the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down in Summit due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical cyclone Hanna, interacted with an approaching cold front from the west, leading to widespread heavy rainfall across the mid Hudson Valley of eastern New York and southwest New England during Saturday September 6th, and ending by early Sunday morning on September 7th. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches fell across this region, leading to some flooding.||In addition, gusty winds occurred across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in the washout of Game Farm Road, located approximately 3 miles east of Pawling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical cyclone Hanna, interacted with an approaching cold front from the west, leading to widespread heavy rainfall across the mid Hudson Valley of eastern New York and southwest New England during Saturday September 6th, and ending by early Sunday morning on September 7th. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches fell across this region, leading to some flooding.||In addition, gusty winds occurred across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Clinton Hollow, along with several other locations within Dutchess County due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong cold frontal passage, and the extratropical remnants of Ike led to strong winds across the southern and western Adirondacks, the western and central Mohawk Valley, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and Schoharie Valley of eastern New York during the early morning hours of Monday September 15th. Measured wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were recorded, leading to numerous downed trees, tree limbs and power lines across the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, large tree limbs and power lines were reported down due to strong winds.||Areas that were most affected included Northumberland, Wilton, and Galway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept east across the region during the evening hours of Saturday October 25th. A wave of low pressure developed along this front and lifted north during the evening, creating locally heavy rainfall across portions of eastern New York and western New England.||In addition, the tight pressure gradient between the approaching front, and strong high pressure off the New England coast, created gusty southeast to south winds across the region from Saturday afternoon into the evening, which led to some downed trees, tree limbs and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires were reported down on Military Road near Bradt Hill Road in Edinburg due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western and central New York was in a very warm tropical air mass during the afternoon and evening as the remnants of Hurricane IKE were approaching the region. Temperatures soared to near record reaching 90 degrees across many interior locations during the late afternoon. IKE���s initial affect on the region came in the form of a line of severe thunderstorms that raced across the area during the late evening and early overnight hours. The damaging winds, measured to 62 mph, downed trees and wires from western New York to the eastern Lake Ontario Region. Specific damage locations included Buffalo, Darien, Fulton and Orleans Four Corners.

A low pressure system developed over the southern Great Lakes on Monday November 24th, and then intensified, reaching the eastern Great Lakes by Tuesday November 25th. A warm front associated with this system produced heavy wet snow across the higher elevations of the southern Adirondacks and southeast Catskills, from late Monday night, into Tuesday afternoon. Total snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches occurred in the highest elevations, especially above 1500 feet.

A developing low pressure system along the northern Gulf Coast on Saturday November 29th traveled north into the lower Ohio Valley by Sunday morning November 30th, and then strengthened upon reaching the eastern Great Lakes by Monday morning on December 1st.||A warm front associated with this system approached from the south during Sunday, producing a widespread light mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain. The precipitation began during the morning hours across the mid Hudson Valley region, and then spread north into the Capital Region around midday, and into the Lake George Saratoga Region, the Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks during the afternoon hours.||Snow and sleet amounts were light, generally an inch or less in the valleys, and 1 to 3 inches across some higher elevations, along with ice accretion of less than one tenth of an inch. However, since this light wintry mix occurred on a busy travel day, the Sunday after Thanksgiving Day, the impact to travelers was high.||Numerous minor accidents were reported, especially across the mid Hudson Valley region during the morning hours, and into the Capital Region and Lake George Saratoga Region during the afternoon and evening.

High pressure brought clear skies, calm winds, and cold air to the region. Temperatures across the area fell below 32 resulting in a widespread freeze and an end to the growing season. Specific readings reported included: 19 in Friendship and Whitesville; 20 in Angelica; 24 in Jamestown; 26 in Wales and Churchville; 27 in Springville and Avon; 28 in Dunkirk, Alden, East Aurora, Pavilion, Dansville and Henrietta; 29 in Mayville, Clarence, Orchard Park, Pittsford, Hilton, Niagara Falls, North Tonawanda, and Geneva; 30 in Amherst and Rochester Airport; 31 in Kenmore, Penfield and Youngstown; and 32 at Buffalo Airport.

A frontal boundary retreated to the north of the region during Friday, July 18th, allowing hot and humid conditions to overspread eastern New York and adjacent western New England. This contributed to the development of numerous thunderstorms during Friday afternoon and evening, some of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and power lines were reported down in Schoharie due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal low produced strong easterly flow into Long Island Sound, causing moderate coastal flooding at Kings Point late on the morning of the 28th. The moderate flood stage of 10.5 feet MLLW was reached at 10:32 am, with a peak tide level of 11.0 feet MLLW at 11:30 am, with tidal levels receding below the moderate bench mark at 12:14 pm. The peak surge from this event was 2.5 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS tidal gauge at Kings Point reported moderate coastal flooding from 10:32 am EDT to 12:14 pm EDT. The peak surge was 2.5 feet, which was obtained by subtracting the observed tide from the predicted tide at the NOS gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong south pressure gradient between a strong offshore high pressure system and an approaching cold front caused south winds to gust from 45 to 55 mph.  Strong winds downed trees and power lines across parts of Southeast New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather station at Cardinal Hayes High School measured a wind gust to 51 mph at 6:39 pm EDT.

An upper atmospheric and surface low remained nearly stationary across Ontario and Quebec on November 26th and 27th. This produced a nearly persistent west to southwest flow of cold, unstable air across relatively mild Lake Ontario waters, which resulted in lake effect snow bands that traversed portions of St. Lawrence county, New York.||Widespread snowfall was 3 to 6 inches in St. Lawrence county and southern Franklin county with several localized bands of more than 6 inches in St. Lawrence county (11 inches in Oswegatchie and 8 inches in Pitcairn).

A sharp cold front crossed the region during the evening hours. The thunderstorms that accompanied the front downed trees and power lines over parts of the Niagara Frontier, especially over Niagara County. Specific reports of damage came from Wilson, Cambria, Lockport and Newfane. Winds were estimated to gust to near 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Massena ASOS recorded 58 knot wind gust at 201 pm EST. Numerous reports of trees down, power lines down and structural damage.

An anomolous low pressure system over southeast New York produced numerous thunderstorms with torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding in portions of New York City, Long Island and the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Jefferson Ave. and Bayville Ave. in Bayville was impassable due to flooding.  Portions of Ludlam Ave. in Bayville and West Shore Rd. in Mill Neck were also impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass spread across the region, causing lake effect snow to develop east of Lake Ontario. The most significant snow fell across the western Mohawk Valley where up to 9 inches was measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow brought 6 to 9 inches of snow to the northern part of the county. Brewerton measured 7 inches and Phoenix received 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system along a nearly stationary boundary intensified as it tracked northeast from western Pennsylvania into central New York from Wednesday morning on July 23rd, through Thursday morning on July 24th. A cold front associated with this low slowly moved east across eastern New York late Wednesday afternoon into early Thursday morning.||The combination of strong upper level dynamics, and abundant moisture in place, led to widespread showers and thunderstorms containing heavy rainfall. This led to flooding across portions of eastern New York from Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding resulted in numerous road closures in portions of Saratoga County. Some of the road closures included State Route 147 and Route 67 near Charlton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 9th, arctic high pressure was located east of New York and New England, but sub-freezing temperatures remained in the northern St. Lawrence Valley due to cold, northeast low-level flow from Quebec. Meanwhile, southwest flow at mid-levels helped deliver mild, moist air into northern New York. ||The mild, moist flow aloft caused the precipitation to initially fall as liquid, but sub-freezing air near the surface allowed the liquid to fall in the form of freezing rain during the evening of the 9th through the morning of the 10th. ||Ice accumulation of 1/4 to just under 1/2 inch was observed in the St. Lawrence River valley of northern St. Lawrence and northern Franklin counties. Numerous vehicle accidents and scattered power outages resulted from this ice accumulation.

An area of high pressure was located across New England and New York on the morning of December 11th.  Meanwhile, complex low pressure that originated in the Mississippi River, traveled north along the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the morning of December 11th.  This low quickly raced up the coast and was located over Long Island by the morning of December 12th and downeast Maine by the afternoon. This system tapped into mild, moist Atlantic air which then traveled above the colder air mass entrenched across the region.||Snow began across the Champlain Valley of New York during the evening of the 11th and briefly mixed with sleet, before precipitation ended as snow by late morning of the 12th. Snowfall accumulations in the Champlain Valley of New York were 4 to 8 inches with some sleet in Essex county.

A cold front moved east across eastern New York and western New England during the late morning through mid afternoon hours of Sunday July 27th. The interaction of this front, and a warm, moist and unstable air mass, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across the eastern Catskills, mid Hudson Valley region, and the central and southern Taconics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home near Accord at approximately 1300 LST. Significant damage was limited to the den and study areas, with smoke damage throughout the house.

A very potent upper atmospheric and surface storm moved across the Great Lakes and into Ontario and Quebec on December 28th. A very strong wind field of 65 to 80 miles per hour, located several thousand feet above the surface, accompanied this storm. A cold front associated with this potent storm, moved across northern New York during the early afternoon and helped transfer some of these strong winds down to the surface.||Strong and damaging wind gusts in excess of 60 mph moved across western and northern New York, including portions of St. Lawrence, Franklin, Essex and Clinton counties. Numerous tree limbs and trees were downed onto roads, power lines and other structures. Several roofs were damaged as well as a few tractor trailers were blown off the road and a small plane was tossed at Potsdam Municipal airport. More than 10,000 residents were without power at the height of the event.

A low pressure system tracked from the southwestern U.S. Thursday morning December 18th, to southern Illinois by Friday morning December 19th, to a position well off the New England coast by Saturday morning December 20th.||This low spread a swath of moderate to heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning Friday afternoon, and ending early Saturday morning.||Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches across eastern New York, including the southeast Catskills and mid Hudson Valley, extending north into the Capital District and across the Lake George Saratoga region into the southern Adirondacks. However, locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches fell across portions of northern Warren County.||This heavy snowfall closed numerous schools and businesses Friday through Saturday, and led to treacherous travel conditions across much of eastern New York State.||One multi-vehicle accident involving a tractor trailer closed the eastbound lanes of the New York State Thruway between exits 25 and 25a, just west of Albany, during Friday afternoon.||In addition, an airplane at Albany International Airport slid into a snowbank and needed to be dug out by airport officials. The plane was taxiing down the runway when the pilot noticed the snowbank and tried to stop. There were no injuries, and plane was not damaged.

A low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes late Saturday December 20th, into Sunday December 21st. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley late Tuesday night December 16th, to Lake Ontario by Wednesday afternoon December 17th, while another low pressure system developed off the Delmarva Peninsula and tracked northeast, passing just east of Cape Cod late Wednesday. This combination of systems brought a swath of light to moderate snowfall to much of east central New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending by midday Wednesday. In addition, sleet and freezing rain also occurred, especially across portions of the southeast Catskills into the mid Hudson Valley region.||Total snow and sleet amounts ranged from an inch or less across the mid Hudson Valley, to 1 to 3 inches across the Capital District and southeast Catskills, with generally 3 to 6 inches occurring across the Mohawk Valley, the Lake George Saratoga region, and southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion of up to one tenth of an inch occurred across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix led to treacherous travel conditions for the Wednesday morning commute across much of eastern New York.

A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. Thunder was even reported in Glens Falls. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending by midday Friday. Total ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from around one half of an inch, up to one inch across portions of the Capital District and the Berkshires. North and west of the Capital District, including the Saratoga region and east central Mohawk Valley, temperatures were colder and frozen precipitation fell. Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches just north and west of the Capital District, while up to 8 to 12 inches of snow and some sleet fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks. ||The ice storm resulted in widespread damage to trees and resultant power outages across eastern New York, where an estimated 220,000 utility customers lost power. Many schools and businesses were shut down for several days due to the loss of power, and impassable roads from extensive fallen debris, resulting in significant economic and societal impacts. States of emergency were declared across large portions of eastern New York.  The hardest hit areas were within the immediate Capital District, across Albany and extreme southern Saratoga Counties, as well as across the central and southern Taconics, from central Rensselaer County into Columbia County and northern Dutchess County.||Bitterly cold temperatures followed in the wake of the storm Saturday and Sunday, compounding the power outages across the region. Numerous warming shelters were setup to assist those who were without power and heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ice accretion from freezing rain of 0.50 to 1.0 inch led to widespread downed trees, tree limbs and power lines. Hardest hit areas included Poestenkill, East Greenbush, Brunswick, Averill Park and Troy.||Approximately 30 to 40 roads were closed across the county due to debris from fallen tree limbs and wires, which included Route 43 near Averill Park.

As this low moved into the eastern Great Lakes, a second low pressure developed south of Long Island Sunday afternoon, and intensified rapidly, moving northeast off the northern New England coast by Monday morning December 22nd.||A swath of moderate to heavy snow fell across the southwest Adirondacks, the Lake George and Saratoga region, the Mohawk and Schoharie Valleys, beginning early Sunday morning, and tapering off by Sunday evening.||Snowfall accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across the southern Adirondacks, where snowfall amounts reached or exceeded one foot.||Strong winds and Lake Effect snowbands persisted in the wake of this storm system for Sunday night and Monday.

A low pressure system tracked from the Gulf of Mexico, the evening of the 10th, through the Carolinas on the 11th, to New England on the 12th. |This storm brought a variety of wintry precipitation to central New York from the morning of the 11th to the morning of the 12th. A band of heavy snow developed across central New York from the Finger Lakes area to Onondaga and Madison counties where between 5 and 10 inches of snow fell. Further east, significant icing occurred across a large part of Chenango, Delaware and Otsego Counties, which received a half inch or more of ice. The rest of central New York received around a quarter inch of ice and from 2 to 6 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 9 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley late Tuesday night December 16th, to Lake Ontario by Wednesday afternoon December 17th, while another low pressure system developed off the Delmarva Peninsula and tracked northeast, passing just east of Cape Cod late Wednesday. This combination of systems brought a swath of light to moderate snowfall to much of east central New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending by midday Wednesday. In addition, sleet and freezing rain also occurred, especially across portions of the southeast Catskills into the mid Hudson Valley region.||Total snow and sleet amounts ranged from an inch or less across the mid Hudson Valley, to 1 to 3 inches across the Capital District and southeast Catskills, with generally 3 to 6 inches occurring across the Mohawk Valley, the Lake George Saratoga region, and southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion of up to one tenth of an inch occurred across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix led to treacherous travel conditions for the Wednesday morning commute across much of eastern New York.

A low pressure system tracked from the southwestern U.S. Thursday morning December 18th, to southern Illinois by Friday morning December 19th, to a position well off the New England coast by Saturday morning December 20th.||This low spread a swath of moderate to heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning Friday afternoon, and ending early Saturday morning.||Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches across eastern New York, including the southeast Catskills and mid Hudson Valley, extending north into the Capital District and across the Lake George Saratoga region into the southern Adirondacks. However, locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches fell across portions of northern Warren County.||This heavy snowfall closed numerous schools and businesses Friday through Saturday, and led to treacherous travel conditions across much of eastern New York State.||One multi-vehicle accident involving a tractor trailer closed the eastbound lanes of the New York State Thruway between exits 25 and 25a, just west of Albany, during Friday afternoon.||In addition, an airplane at Albany International Airport slid into a snowbank and needed to be dug out by airport officials. The plane was taxiing down the runway when the pilot noticed the snowbank and tried to stop. There were no injuries, and plane was not damaged.

A low pressure system tracked from the southwest United States to the Plains, Mid-West, and the middle Atlantic states from late on the 18th to the evening of December 19th. The storm spread snow across all of central New York, with accumulations of about 6 to 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 7 inches of snow fell across parts of the county, with general snowfall amounts averaging 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. Thunder was even reported in Glens Falls. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending by midday Friday. Total ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from around one half of an inch, up to one inch across portions of the Capital District and the Berkshires. North and west of the Capital District, including the Saratoga region and east central Mohawk Valley, temperatures were colder and frozen precipitation fell. Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches just north and west of the Capital District, while up to 8 to 12 inches of snow and some sleet fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks. ||The ice storm resulted in widespread damage to trees and resultant power outages across eastern New York, where an estimated 220,000 utility customers lost power. Many schools and businesses were shut down for several days due to the loss of power, and impassable roads from extensive fallen debris, resulting in significant economic and societal impacts. States of emergency were declared across large portions of eastern New York.  The hardest hit areas were within the immediate Capital District, across Albany and extreme southern Saratoga Counties, as well as across the central and southern Taconics, from central Rensselaer County into Columbia County and northern Dutchess County.||Bitterly cold temperatures followed in the wake of the storm Saturday and Sunday, compounding the power outages across the region. Numerous warming shelters were setup to assist those who were without power and heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, mixed with some sleet and freezing rain, fell across Hamilton County from Thursday evening into midday Friday.||Snow and sleet amounts of 6 to 8 inches were reported, with 8 inches at Indian Lake, and 6 inches at Wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over the eastern Great Lakes on Tuesday morning, January 1st, tracked east across central New York Tuesday afternoon, and off the New England coast by Wednesday morning on January 2nd.||As this low pressure tracked east, a swath of light to moderate snow fell across much of eastern New York and western New England.||This storm system produced 3 to 8 inches of snow accumulation across eastern New York State, with the greatest amounts across higher elevations of the eastern Catskills, and portions of the upper Hudson River Valley.||In the wake of this storm system, persistent snow showers continued into Wednesday morning. Furthermore, smaller bands of moderate to locally heavy snowfall affected portions of the Capital District and upper Hudson Valley, producing an additional 2 to 5 inches of snowfall Wednesday morning.||This snowfall created hazardous travel conditions for the Wednesday morning commute, and also led to the delayed openings of some schools and businesses Wednesday morning.

Low pressure over the eastern Gulf of Mexico on the evening of December 10, tracked northeast and up into the Carolinas by the following evening of December 11.  In response to a strong upper trough approaching from the west, a secondary low developed along the Mid Atlantic coast during the evening and rapidly deepened overnight. By the following morning, the low had deepened to an estimated 994 mb, just to the south and east of Montauk Point. A transient high over eastern Canada provided enough cold air across northern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley for a prolonged period of freezing rain during the late morning hours of December 11 through the early morning hours of December 12. Thereafter, enough warm air worked in for a complete changeover to rain with several inches of rainfall.||Automated observations in tandem with pictures from trained spotters supported ice accumulations around one half inch across the northern half of Orange county.  ||At 0800EST, there was a major automobile accident on the Newburgh-Beacon Bridge in Orange County with eight injuries. There were also scattered reports of trees and power lines down with the American Red Cross opening a shelter in Central Valley the following day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell, starting Tuesday morning, and tapering off Tuesday afternoon.||Several inches of snow accumulated, creating treacherous travel conditions Tuesday evening into Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. Thunder was even reported in Glens Falls. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending by midday Friday. Total ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from around one half of an inch, up to one inch across portions of the Capital District and the Berkshires. North and west of the Capital District, including the Saratoga region and east central Mohawk Valley, temperatures were colder and frozen precipitation fell. Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches just north and west of the Capital District, while up to 8 to 12 inches of snow and some sleet fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks. ||The ice storm resulted in widespread damage to trees and resultant power outages across eastern New York, where an estimated 220,000 utility customers lost power. Many schools and businesses were shut down for several days due to the loss of power, and impassable roads from extensive fallen debris, resulting in significant economic and societal impacts. States of emergency were declared across large portions of eastern New York.  The hardest hit areas were within the immediate Capital District, across Albany and extreme southern Saratoga Counties, as well as across the central and southern Taconics, from central Rensselaer County into Columbia County and northern Dutchess County.||Bitterly cold temperatures followed in the wake of the storm Saturday and Sunday, compounding the power outages across the region. Numerous warming shelters were setup to assist those who were without power and heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant wintry mix of snow, sleet and some freezing rain occurred from late Thursday afternoon, into midday Friday.||Snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches, in addition to ice accretion of up to one quarter of an inch from freezing rain.

Low pressure located over the eastern Great Lakes on Tuesday morning, January 1st, tracked east across central New York Tuesday afternoon, and off the New England coast by Wednesday morning on January 2nd.||As this low pressure tracked east, a swath of light to moderate snow fell across much of eastern New York and western New England.||This storm system produced 3 to 8 inches of snow accumulation across eastern New York State, with the greatest amounts across higher elevations of the eastern Catskills, and portions of the upper Hudson River Valley.||In the wake of this storm system, persistent snow showers continued into Wednesday morning. Furthermore, smaller bands of moderate to locally heavy snowfall affected portions of the Capital District and upper Hudson Valley, producing an additional 2 to 5 inches of snowfall Wednesday morning.||This snowfall created hazardous travel conditions for the Wednesday morning commute, and also led to the delayed openings of some schools and businesses Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began Tuesday morning, and ended by early Wednesday morning. Several inches of snowfall accumulated. This created hazardous travel conditions Tuesday evening, into the Wednesday morning commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low tracked from the Midwest during the morning of December 19, to off the southern New Jersey coast in the evening. At the same time, a large, sprawling high over eastern Canada estimated at 1035 mb nosed southward along the lee of the Appalachians. The combination of the two features provided a strong overrunning event with widespread snowfall across southeast New York.  ||Snowfall amounts averaged between 6 and 10 inches, with the highest amounts occurring across northern portions of Orange county. The following are snowfall ranges across the area: Bronx County, 6.0 inches in the Bronx to 7.0 inches in Pelham Bay Park; Orange County, 4.3 inches in New Milford to 10.0 inches in Monroe, Newburgh, Highland Mills and Fort Montgomery; Putnam County, 8.5 inches in Mahopac; Rockland County, 5.0 inches in Nyack to 7.1 inches in Piermont; and Westchester County, 4.0 inches in Rye Brook to 8.0 inches in Croton-on-Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air pouring over the relatively warm waters of the the Great Lakes led to locally intense snowbands across portions of Hamilton and Northern Herkimer Counties. Snowfall amounts of up to one foot fell within the core of the most persistent bands, near North Wilmurt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense snowband oscillated across northern Herkimer County Sunday night into Monday morning, producing 6 to 12 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air pouring over the relatively warm waters of the the Great Lakes led to locally intense snowbands across portions of Hamilton and Northern Herkimer Counties. Snowfall amounts of up to one foot fell within the core of the most persistent bands, near North Wilmurt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense snowband oscillated across northern and central Hamilton County Sunday night into Monday morning, producing 6 to 8 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the southern Great Lakes on Monday November 24th, and then intensified, reaching the eastern Great Lakes by Tuesday November 25th. A warm front associated with this system produced heavy wet snow across the higher elevations of the southern Adirondacks and southeast Catskills, from late Monday night, into Tuesday afternoon. Total snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches occurred in the highest elevations, especially above 1500 feet.

A low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes late Saturday December 20th, into Sunday December 21st. As this low moved into the eastern Great Lakes, a second low pressure developed south of Long Island Sunday afternoon, and intensified rapidly, moving northeast off the northern New England coast by Monday morning December 22nd.||A swath of moderate to heavy snow fell across the southwest Adirondacks, the Lake George and Saratoga region, the Mohawk and Schoharie Valleys, beginning early Sunday morning, and tapering off by Sunday evening.||Snowfall accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across the southern Adirondacks, where snowfall amounts reached or exceeded one foot.||Strong winds and Lake Effect snowbands persisted in the wake of this storm system for Sunday night and Monday.

Low pressure tracked northeast from the Gulf of Mexico and over the local area along a stationary boundary from December 11th through December 12th. A prolonged period of rain, which was heavy at times, caused widespread flooding across Southeast New York with total rainfall amounts ranging from 2.5 inches to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montauk Highway was closed in Shinnecock Hills due to major flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked northeast from the Gulf of Mexico and over the local area along a stationary boundary from December 11th through December 12th. A prolonged period of rain, which was heavy at times, caused widespread flooding across Southeast New York with total rainfall amounts ranging from 2.5 inches to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern State Pkwy. was closed at the Cross Island Pkwy. due to extensive roadway flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southwest United States to the Plains, Mid-West, and the middle Atlantic states from late on the 18th to the evening of December 19th. The storm spread snow across all of central New York, with accumulations of about 6 to 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 7 inches of snow fell across parts of the county, with general snowfall amounts averaging 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southwest United States to the Plains, Mid-West, and the middle Atlantic states from late on the 18th to the evening of December 19th. The storm spread snow across all of central New York, with accumulations of about 6 to 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 9 inches of snow fell across parts of the county, with general snowfall amounts averaging 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley late Tuesday night December 16th, to Lake Ontario by Wednesday afternoon December 17th, while another low pressure system developed off the Delmarva Peninsula and tracked northeast, passing just east of Cape Cod late Wednesday. This combination of systems brought a swath of light to moderate snowfall to much of east central New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending by midday Wednesday. In addition, sleet and freezing rain also occurred, especially across portions of the southeast Catskills into the mid Hudson Valley region.||Total snow and sleet amounts ranged from an inch or less across the mid Hudson Valley, to 1 to 3 inches across the Capital District and southeast Catskills, with generally 3 to 6 inches occurring across the Mohawk Valley, the Lake George Saratoga region, and southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion of up to one tenth of an inch occurred across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix led to treacherous travel conditions for the Wednesday morning commute across much of eastern New York.

A low pressure system tracked from the southwestern U.S. Thursday morning December 18th, to southern Illinois by Friday morning December 19th, to a position well off the New England coast by Saturday morning December 20th.||This low spread a swath of moderate to heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning Friday afternoon, and ending early Saturday morning.||Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches across eastern New York, including the southeast Catskills and mid Hudson Valley, extending north into the Capital District and across the Lake George Saratoga region into the southern Adirondacks. However, locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches fell across portions of northern Warren County.||This heavy snowfall closed numerous schools and businesses Friday through Saturday, and led to treacherous travel conditions across much of eastern New York State.||One multi-vehicle accident involving a tractor trailer closed the eastbound lanes of the New York State Thruway between exits 25 and 25a, just west of Albany, during Friday afternoon.||In addition, an airplane at Albany International Airport slid into a snowbank and needed to be dug out by airport officials. The plane was taxiing down the runway when the pilot noticed the snowbank and tried to stop. There were no injuries, and plane was not damaged.

A low pressure system tracked rapidly northeast, from the Great Lakes Wednesday morning on December 24th, to off the northern New England coast by Thursday morning on December 25th.||A warm front associated with this low approached eastern New York and western New England during Wednesday, spreading a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain during the morning hours, before changing to plain rain by late Wednesday afternoon.||This wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions during the Wednesday morning commute.||Snow and sleet amounts of 1 to 4 inches fell across the Mohawk Valley, the southern Adirondacks, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and the upper Hudson Valley, with generally less than an inch elsewhere. In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain of up to one tenth of an inch occurred, especially within the immediate Capital District.

A low pressure system tracked quickly east from the Ohio Valley, across Pennsylvania before strengthening off the coast near Long Island. This system spread snow across central New York, with snow amounts ranging from 5 to 10 inches across much of the Finger Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 10 inches across the county. Cazenovia measured 10.5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 10 inches across the northern third of Oneida County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes late Saturday December 20th, into Sunday December 21st. As this low moved into the eastern Great Lakes, a second low pressure developed south of Long Island Sunday afternoon, and intensified rapidly, moving northeast off the northern New England coast by Monday morning December 22nd.||A swath of moderate to heavy snow fell across the southwest Adirondacks, the Lake George and Saratoga region, the Mohawk and Schoharie Valleys, beginning early Sunday morning, and tapering off by Sunday evening.||Snowfall accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across the southern Adirondacks, where snowfall amounts reached or exceeded one foot.||Strong winds and Lake Effect snowbands persisted in the wake of this storm system for Sunday night and Monday.

A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending early Friday afternoon.||Total liquid amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell across the Capital Region, eastern Mohawk Valley, the Saratoga region, and upper Hudson Valley, with 2 to 4 inches common across much of the eastern Catskills, mid Hudson Valley and southern Taconics, with the greatest amounts across higher southeast facing terrain of the Catskills.||This heavy rain led to flooding of small streams and creeks across the region, especially late Thursday night into Friday morning, in addition to widespread ponding of water in urban areas due to ice blocking storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to widespread ponding of water across southern and central Saratoga County. This was exacerbated by clogged storm drains, which were blocked by ice.||Areas which suffered from the greatest ponding of water included Clifton Park and Malta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley late Tuesday night December 16th, to Lake Ontario by Wednesday afternoon December 17th, while another low pressure system developed off the Delmarva Peninsula and tracked northeast, passing just east of Cape Cod late Wednesday. This combination of systems brought a swath of light to moderate snowfall to much of east central New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending by midday Wednesday. In addition, sleet and freezing rain also occurred, especially across portions of the southeast Catskills into the mid Hudson Valley region.||Total snow and sleet amounts ranged from an inch or less across the mid Hudson Valley, to 1 to 3 inches across the Capital District and southeast Catskills, with generally 3 to 6 inches occurring across the Mohawk Valley, the Lake George Saratoga region, and southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion of up to one tenth of an inch occurred across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix led to treacherous travel conditions for the Wednesday morning commute across much of eastern New York.

A low pressure system tracked from the Gulf of Mexico, the evening of the 10th, through the Carolinas on the 11th, to New England on the 12th. |This storm brought a variety of wintry precipitation to central New York from the morning of the 11th to the morning of the 12th. A band of heavy snow developed across central New York from the Finger Lakes area to Onondaga and Madison counties where between 5 and 10 inches of snow fell. Further east, significant icing occurred across a large part of Chenango, Delaware and Otsego Counties, which received a half inch or more of ice. The rest of central New York received around a quarter inch of ice and from 2 to 6 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from 4 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southwestern U.S. Thursday morning December 18th, to southern Illinois by Friday morning December 19th, to a position well off the New England coast by Saturday morning December 20th.||This low spread a swath of moderate to heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning Friday afternoon, and ending early Saturday morning.||Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches across eastern New York, including the southeast Catskills and mid Hudson Valley, extending north into the Capital District and across the Lake George Saratoga region into the southern Adirondacks. However, locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches fell across portions of northern Warren County.||This heavy snowfall closed numerous schools and businesses Friday through Saturday, and led to treacherous travel conditions across much of eastern New York State.||One multi-vehicle accident involving a tractor trailer closed the eastbound lanes of the New York State Thruway between exits 25 and 25a, just west of Albany, during Friday afternoon.||In addition, an airplane at Albany International Airport slid into a snowbank and needed to be dug out by airport officials. The plane was taxiing down the runway when the pilot noticed the snowbank and tried to stop. There were no injuries, and plane was not damaged.

A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. Thunder was even reported in Glens Falls. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending by midday Friday. Total ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from around one half of an inch, up to one inch across portions of the Capital District and the Berkshires. North and west of the Capital District, including the Saratoga region and east central Mohawk Valley, temperatures were colder and frozen precipitation fell. Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches just north and west of the Capital District, while up to 8 to 12 inches of snow and some sleet fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks. ||The ice storm resulted in widespread damage to trees and resultant power outages across eastern New York, where an estimated 220,000 utility customers lost power. Many schools and businesses were shut down for several days due to the loss of power, and impassable roads from extensive fallen debris, resulting in significant economic and societal impacts. States of emergency were declared across large portions of eastern New York.  The hardest hit areas were within the immediate Capital District, across Albany and extreme southern Saratoga Counties, as well as across the central and southern Taconics, from central Rensselaer County into Columbia County and northern Dutchess County.||Bitterly cold temperatures followed in the wake of the storm Saturday and Sunday, compounding the power outages across the region. Numerous warming shelters were setup to assist those who were without power and heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion from freezing rain of one half to one inch occurred, resulting in widespread downed trees, tree limbs and power lines across the county.||The hardest hit areas included New Paltz and Kingston. Approximately 140 power lines were reported down across the county.

A strong cold front crossed the region Thursday, December 4th and ushered in a cold westerly flow of air across New York. As the cold westerly flow set up across the warmer waters of Lake Ontario, a band of lake effect snow became organized across northern Oneida county Thursday evening, the 4th, and lasted until Friday morning. Total snow accumulations ranged up to 9 inches. The heaviest snow fell generally west of interstate 26 in the towns of Westdale and Camden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect snow brought up to nine inches of snow to the county. The heaviest snow fell generally west of interstate 26 in the towns of Westdale and Camden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure tracked from the eastern Great Lakes to the maritimes of Canada from the evening of the 6th to the evening of the 7th. The area of low pressure strengthened into a major winter storm drawing cold westerly winds into New York state. The cold westerly winds across the warmer waters of Lake Ontario brought a band of lake effect snow across Oneida county on the 7th. The heaviest snow fell in the northern parts of the county where up to 10 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated nearly 10 inches across parts of Northern Oneida county. Point Rock measured 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. Thunder was even reported in Glens Falls. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending by midday Friday. Total ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from around one half of an inch, up to one inch across portions of the Capital District and the Berkshires. North and west of the Capital District, including the Saratoga region and east central Mohawk Valley, temperatures were colder and frozen precipitation fell. Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches just north and west of the Capital District, while up to 8 to 12 inches of snow and some sleet fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks. ||The ice storm resulted in widespread damage to trees and resultant power outages across eastern New York, where an estimated 220,000 utility customers lost power. Many schools and businesses were shut down for several days due to the loss of power, and impassable roads from extensive fallen debris, resulting in significant economic and societal impacts. States of emergency were declared across large portions of eastern New York.  The hardest hit areas were within the immediate Capital District, across Albany and extreme southern Saratoga Counties, as well as across the central and southern Taconics, from central Rensselaer County into Columbia County and northern Dutchess County.||Bitterly cold temperatures followed in the wake of the storm Saturday and Sunday, compounding the power outages across the region. Numerous warming shelters were setup to assist those who were without power and heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ice accretion from freezing rain of 0.50 to 1.0 inch led to widespread downed trees, tree limbs and power lines. Hardest hit areas included Clifton Park and Halfmoon.||Several trees were also reported down alongside the Adirondack Northway, Interstate 87, between exits 9 and 11.

A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley late Tuesday night December 16th, to Lake Ontario by Wednesday afternoon December 17th, while another low pressure system developed off the Delmarva Peninsula and tracked northeast, passing just east of Cape Cod late Wednesday. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. Thunder was even reported in Glens Falls. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending by midday Friday. Total ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from around one half of an inch, up to one inch across portions of the Capital District and the Berkshires. North and west of the Capital District, including the Saratoga region and east central Mohawk Valley, temperatures were colder and frozen precipitation fell. Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches just north and west of the Capital District, while up to 8 to 12 inches of snow and some sleet fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks. ||The ice storm resulted in widespread damage to trees and resultant power outages across eastern New York, where an estimated 220,000 utility customers lost power. Many schools and businesses were shut down for several days due to the loss of power, and impassable roads from extensive fallen debris, resulting in significant economic and societal impacts. States of emergency were declared across large portions of eastern New York.  The hardest hit areas were within the immediate Capital District, across Albany and extreme southern Saratoga Counties, as well as across the central and southern Taconics, from central Rensselaer County into Columbia County and northern Dutchess County.||Bitterly cold temperatures followed in the wake of the storm Saturday and Sunday, compounding the power outages across the region. Numerous warming shelters were setup to assist those who were without power and heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion from freezing rain of one half to three quarters of an inch occurred, resulting in widespread downed trees, tree limbs and power lines.||The hardest hit areas included Coxsackie and Athens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southwestern U.S. Thursday morning December 18th, to southern Illinois by Friday morning December 19th, to a position well off the New England coast by Saturday morning December 20th.||This low spread a swath of moderate to heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning Friday afternoon, and ending early Saturday morning.||Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches across eastern New York, including the southeast Catskills and mid Hudson Valley, extending north into the Capital District and across the Lake George Saratoga region into the southern Adirondacks. However, locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches fell across portions of northern Warren County.||This heavy snowfall closed numerous schools and businesses Friday through Saturday, and led to treacherous travel conditions across much of eastern New York State.||One multi-vehicle accident involving a tractor trailer closed the eastbound lanes of the New York State Thruway between exits 25 and 25a, just west of Albany, during Friday afternoon.||In addition, an airplane at Albany International Airport slid into a snowbank and needed to be dug out by airport officials. The plane was taxiing down the runway when the pilot noticed the snowbank and tried to stop. There were no injuries, and plane was not damaged.

A low pressure system tracked rapidly northeast, from the Great Lakes Wednesday morning on December 24th, to off the northern New England coast by Thursday morning on December 25th.||A warm front associated with this low approached eastern New York and western New England during Wednesday, spreading a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain during the morning hours, before changing to plain rain by late Wednesday afternoon.||This wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions during the Wednesday morning commute.||Snow and sleet amounts of 1 to 4 inches fell across the Mohawk Valley, the southern Adirondacks, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and the upper Hudson Valley, with generally less than an inch elsewhere. In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain of up to one tenth of an inch occurred, especially within the immediate Capital District.

A low pressure system tracked quickly east from the Ohio Valley, across Pennsylvania before strengthening off the coast near Long Island. This system spread snow across central New York, with snow amounts ranging from 5 to 10 inches across much of the Finger Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 11 inches across the southern two-thirds of Oneida county. Sauquoit received 10.9 inches, Cassville 10.0 inches, and Utica measured 7.0 inches.

A low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes late Saturday December 20th, into Sunday December 21st. As this low moved into the eastern Great Lakes, a second low pressure developed south of Long Island Sunday afternoon, and intensified rapidly, moving northeast off the northern New England coast by Monday morning December 22nd.||A swath of moderate to heavy snow fell across the southwest Adirondacks, the Lake George and Saratoga region, the Mohawk and Schoharie Valleys, beginning early Sunday morning, and tapering off by Sunday evening.||Snowfall accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with the greatest amounts occurring across the southern Adirondacks, where snowfall amounts reached or exceeded one foot.||Strong winds and Lake Effect snowbands persisted in the wake of this storm system for Sunday night and Monday.

A low pressure system tracked from the southwest United States to the Plains, Mid-West, and the middle Atlantic states from late on the 18th to the evening of December 19th. The storm spread snow across all of central New York, with accumulations of about 6 to 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 10 inches of snow fell across parts of the county, with general snowfall amounts averaging 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley late Tuesday night December 16th, to Lake Ontario by Wednesday afternoon December 17th, while another low pressure system developed off the Delmarva Peninsula and tracked northeast, passing just east of Cape Cod late Wednesday. This combination of systems brought a swath of light to moderate snowfall to much of east central New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending by midday Wednesday. In addition, sleet and freezing rain also occurred, especially across portions of the southeast Catskills into the mid Hudson Valley region.||Total snow and sleet amounts ranged from an inch or less across the mid Hudson Valley, to 1 to 3 inches across the Capital District and southeast Catskills, with generally 3 to 6 inches occurring across the Mohawk Valley, the Lake George Saratoga region, and southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion of up to one tenth of an inch occurred across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix led to treacherous travel conditions for the Wednesday morning commute across much of eastern New York.

However, locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches fell across portions of northern Warren County.||This heavy snowfall closed numerous schools and businesses Friday through Saturday, and led to treacherous travel conditions across much of eastern New York State.||One multi-vehicle accident involving a tractor trailer closed the eastbound lanes of the New York State Thruway between exits 25 and 25a, just west of Albany, during Friday afternoon.||In addition, an airplane at Albany International Airport slid into a snowbank and needed to be dug out by airport officials. The plane was taxiing down the runway when the pilot noticed the snowbank and tried to stop. There were no injuries, and plane was not damaged.

A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley late Tuesday night December 16th, to Lake Ontario by Wednesday afternoon December 17th, while another low pressure system developed off the Delmarva Peninsula and tracked northeast, passing just east of Cape Cod late Wednesday. A low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes late Saturday December 20th, into Sunday December 21st. The combination of cold temperatures, and brisk winds, resulted in wind chill values of -15 to -20 F across the higher elevations of the eastern Catskills during the early morning hours of Monday December 22nd.

An area of low pressure moved east across the Great Lakes, while a second low developed off the coast of New Jersey. Theses systems combined to bring mainly light snow across central New York, with higher amounts across northern and northeast parts of the state. Northern Oneida county picked up around 7 inches of snow from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought around 7 inches of snow to the northern sections of Oneida county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect snow dropped south to areas east of Lake Ontario early in the morning of the 22nd, then southward to the New York State Thruway corridor by daybreak. Snow fell heavy at times through the day, before the band lifted back to the north again late in the evening. Total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches in Oneida County to between 10 and 20 inches in Onondaga and Madison Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow fell across the county, with amounts ranging from 5 to almost 19 inches. Lakeport received 18.5 inches.

A strong cold front swept from west to east Saturday evening, preceded and accompanied by showers and isolated thunderstorms, some of which contained strong wind gusts and a brief period of very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of heavy rain led to significant ponding of water in Delmar, on Kenwood Avenue between Adams Street and Cherry Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across eastern New York and western New England on Wednesday, December 10th, ushering a cold airmass into the region. A low pressure system developed over the southeast states Wednesday and Wednesday night. This storm then headed northeast Thursday and Thursday night, December 11th, with precipitation spreading northward well in advance of the low. The low continued to track northeast, passing over the mid Atlantic region late Thursday night, and over the New York City Metropolitan area and southern New England Friday morning, December 12th. The low then moved to the Canadian Maritimes Friday night. ||As the storm approached, warmer air moved in aloft, and with a cold airmass remaining in place at the surface, this set the stage for a significant mixed precipitation event. The precipitation came down heavy at times, especially Thursday night. Hourly precipitation rates of one quarter to one third of an inch were reported for several hours in the form of freezing rain across much of the region. Thunder was even reported in Glens Falls. The precipitation changed back to snow before ending by midday Friday. Total ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from around one half of an inch, up to one inch across portions of the Capital District and the Berkshires. North and west of the Capital District, including the Saratoga region and east central Mohawk Valley, temperatures were colder and frozen precipitation fell. Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches just north and west of the Capital District, while up to 8 to 12 inches of snow and some sleet fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks. ||The ice storm resulted in widespread damage to trees and resultant power outages across eastern New York, where an estimated 220,000 utility customers lost power. Many schools and businesses were shut down for several days due to the loss of power, and impassable roads from extensive fallen debris, resulting in significant economic and societal impacts. States of emergency were declared across large portions of eastern New York.  The hardest hit areas were within the immediate Capital District, across Albany and extreme southern Saratoga Counties, as well as across the central and southern Taconics, from central Rensselaer County into Columbia County and northern Dutchess County.||Bitterly cold temperatures followed in the wake of the storm Saturday and Sunday, compounding the power outages across the region. Numerous warming shelters were setup to assist those who were without power and heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion from freezing rain of one half to one inch occurred, resulting in widespread downed trees, tree limbs and power lines across the county. Higher elevations across central portions of the county were hardest hit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southwest United States to the Plains, Mid-West, and the middle Atlantic states from late on the 18th to the evening of December 19th. The storm spread snow across all of central New York, with accumulations of about 6 to 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 8 inches of snow fell across parts of the county, with general snowfall amounts averaging 6 to 8 inches.

A strong and rapidly intensifying low pressure system moved from the Great Lakes across Ontario and western Quebec during the morning and early afternoon of January 9th. A very strong low-level wind field with southwest winds in excess of 70 mph just above the surface moved across northern New York during the late morning and early afternoon hours. ||These strong winds translated down to the surface and caused widespread wind damage in the form of numerous downed trees, tractor trailers blown over as well as structural damage to buildings throughout the St. Lawrence River Valley and northern Adirondacks. Some of this structural damage included portions of roofs blown off, snapped power poles, fallen trees on houses and vehicles, as well as the collapse of an barn and other buildings. Some of the greater impacted communities included |Massena, Oswegatchie, Potsdam, Brasher (St. Lawrence county) as well as Duane, Malone and Bellmont (Franklin county) where a barn and building collapsed from the damaging winds. Thousands of residents lost power in Clinton, Essex, Franklin and St. Lawrence counties with temporary shelters opened throughout St. Lawrence and Franklin counties.||Some of the higher observed wind gusts included; Malone (Franklin county) at 68 mph, Massena (St. Lawrence county) at 66 mph and Potsdam (St. Lawrence county) with 59 mph. However, structural damage in Ogdensburg, Massena and Potsdam suggest wind gusts estimated around 80 mph and there was a measured 79 mph wind gust at Fort Drum in nearby Jefferson county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at Massena International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front crossed the region during early morning hours. The thunderstorms that accompanied the front produced damaging wind gusts measured to 75 mph. Trees and power lines were downed by the winds throughout the region. At the Buffalo Airport, a Boeing 737 on the ground was blown into a jet way and damaged. Several homes and automobiles sustained damage by falling trees and limbs. In Watertown, a roof was blown off a building.

A low pressure system approaching from the Great Lakes, in combination with another low pressure system traveling northeast off the mid Atlantic coast, produced moderate to locally heavy snowfall across portions of eastern New York and western New England from late Sunday night on January 13th, into Monday morning on January 14th. ||Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches, with the greatest snowfall amounts across portions of eastern Dutchess County.||This snowfall created hazardous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute, and also led to the closings, or delayed openings of numerous schools and businesses for Monday morning.
