In addition, some road closures were reported due to the fallen debris.||Some of the hardest hit areas included Delanson in western Schenectady county, Dresden Center in northern Washington county, and Richmondville in western Schoharie county.||Further south and east, ice accretions generally were between one quarter, to less than one half of an inch, including across much of the Mohawk Valley, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and into the immediate Capital District.

Arctic high pressure was located across the Great Lakes on March 7th and retreated across Ontario province Canada on March 8th. Meanwhile, a deep upper atmospheric trough lifted a mild and very moist surface low from the Gulf of Mexico on the 7th across New England on the 8th. The clash between air masses and abundant moisture accounted for a heavy precipitation event across northern New York.||The close proximity to the deeper, arctic air across Ontario province accounted for a snow mixed with sleet at the onset on the night of the 7th that changed to mainly snow on the 8th across the St. Lawrence River Valley. Meanwhile, rain overspread the Champlain Valley during the evening of March 7th with scattered areas of freezing rain and sleet along the Canadian border. On March 8th, colder air filtered into the Champlain Valley near the surface, which changed precipitation to a prolonged period of heavy freezing rain and sleet that ended during the evening.||Sleet and freezing rain caused havoc across the Champlain Valley of New York with an inch or more of sleet as well as an half inch or more of ice accumulation from freezing rain. While, heavy snow and sleet accumulation in the Adirondacks and St. Lawrence River Valley caused equal amounts of problems.||Numerous vehicle accidents, downed tree limbs, power lines and power outages impacted portions of northern New York.

Showers and a few thunderstorms developed out ahead of a cold frontal boundary. There were isolated severe thunderstorms east of Lake Ontario, and one of these storms produced a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail fell for one minute. Also, pea size hail fell for 8 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably cold air mass, clear skies and light winds led to widespread freezing temperatures, along with frost across the mid Hudson Valley, Capital District, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and Mohawk Valley of eastern New York during the early morning hours of Thursday May 1st.||The freeze and frost was particularly damaging to fruit trees, as a previous warm spell in mid to late April led to unusually advanced stages of bloom. Preliminary damage estimates were near 20 million dollars in production losses, mainly for apple and peach orchards across the mid Hudson Valley region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass, clear skies and light winds led to a widespread frost, along with subfreezing temperatures during the early morning hours of May 1st. These cold temperatures, combined with the anomalously early blossoming of vegetation due to an early season warm spell, had significant agricultural impact for portions of southeast Warren County.

Rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches and melting snowfall caused flooding of some roadways, basements, and smaller creeks.

The melting of a deep snowpack led to moderate or higher river flooding across portions of the upper Hudson River basin, including the Schroon River, Lake Champlain, and the Great Sacandaga Lake.||Snow water equivalents of nearly one foot began to melt during a warm period beginning around April 10th, and led to river flooding which persisted through the end of April.||Along the Schroon River, at least one permanent home, along with a few summer camps were flooded. On the Hudson River near Schuylerville, a sod farm suffered estimated losses of $225,000 due to prolonged submersion of sod in Hudson River flood waters. In addition, the New York State Canal Corporation was unable to open the Hudson-Champlain Canal in April due to high water levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The melting of a deep snowpack led to moderate or higher river flooding across portions of the upper Hudson River basin, including the Great Sacandaga Lake, which crested at a record level.||Snow water equivalents of nearly one foot began to melt during a warm period beginning around April 10th, and led to river flooding which persisted through the end of April.||As the water levels rose on the Great Sacandaga Lake, several homes and businesses had water enter basements. In addition, the New York State Canal Corporation was unable to open the Hudson-Champlain Canal in April due to high water levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast across southern Ontario and Quebec Canada, bringing numerous showers and thunderstorms across upstate New York. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.

A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was also reported.

The melting of a deep snowpack led to moderate or higher river flooding across portions of the upper Hudson River basin, including the Schroon River, Lake Champlain, and the Great Sacandaga Lake.||Snow water equivalents of nearly one foot began to melt during a warm period beginning around April 10th, and led to river flooding which persisted through the end of April.||Along the Schroon River, at least one permanent home, along with a few summer camps were flooded. On the Hudson River near Schuylerville, a sod farm suffered estimated losses of $225,000 due to prolonged submersion of sod in Hudson River flood waters. In addition, the New York State Canal Corporation was unable to open the Hudson-Champlain Canal in April due to high water levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The melting of a deep snowpack led to moderate or higher river flooding across portions of the upper Hudson River basin, including the Great Sacandaga Lake, and the Hudson River between Schuylerville and Mechanicville.||Snow water equivalents of nearly one foot began to melt during a warm period beginning around April 10th, and led to river flooding which persisted through the end of April.||On the Hudson River near Schuylerville, a sod farm suffered estimated losses of $225,000 due to prolonged submersion of sod in Hudson River flood waters. In addition, the New York State Canal Corporation was unable to open the Hudson-Champlain Canal in April due to high water levels. Furthermore, the crest of the Great Sacandaga Lake level was a new record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Approximately 3100 acres of the Minnewaska State Park in Ulster County burned as a result of a brush fire which spread due to gusty winds and dry brush. The initial fire was reported on Thursday April 17th, at approximately 1130 LST, and was 80 percent contained by Tuesday April 22nd.||At the peak of the fire, flames burned up to 100 feet high, and forced the closure of portions of Routes 44 and 55 which traverses the park. The area consisted mainly of hardwood forest and pine barrens on the Shawangunk Ridge, which contained steep slopes and cliffs. The fire was located mainly across the eastern portion of the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably cool air mass, mainly clear skies and light winds led to frost formation across portions of eastern New York and western New England during the early morning hours of Thursday May 29th. The most widespread frost occurred across portions of the southern and western Adirondacks, the Schoharie Valley, and the higher terrain immediately east of the Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of frost occurred during the early morning hours of Thursday May 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably cold air mass, clear skies and light winds led to widespread freezing temperatures, along with frost across the mid Hudson Valley, Capital District, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and Mohawk Valley of eastern New York during the early morning hours of Thursday May 1st.||The freeze and frost was particularly damaging to fruit trees, as a previous warm spell in mid to late April led to unusually advanced stages of bloom. Preliminary damage estimates were near 20 million dollars in production losses, mainly for apple and peach orchards across the mid Hudson Valley region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass, clear skies and light winds led to a widespread frost, along with subfreezing temperatures during the early morning hours of May 1st. These cold temperatures, combined with the anomalously early blossoming of vegetation due to an early season warm spell, had significant agricultural impact for portions of southern Fulton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably cold air mass, clear skies and light winds led to widespread freezing temperatures, along with frost across the mid Hudson Valley, Capital District, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and Mohawk Valley of eastern New York during the early morning hours of Thursday May 1st.||The freeze and frost was particularly damaging to fruit trees, as a previous warm spell in mid to late April led to unusually advanced stages of bloom. Preliminary damage estimates were near 20 million dollars in production losses, mainly for apple and peach orchards across the mid Hudson Valley region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass, clear skies and light winds led to a widespread frost, along with subfreezing temperatures during the early morning hours of May 1st. These cold temperatures, combined with the anomalously early blossoming of vegetation due to an early season warm spell, had significant agricultural impact for portions of southern Saratoga County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported at least 50 downed trees, and up to 12 power poles snapped due to strong thunderstorm winds near Glenerie. In addition, at least 5 homes sustained minor damage due to the fallen trees.||A National Weather Service storm survey team determined straight line winds from a microburst within a thunderstorm as the cause of the damage. The microburst encompassed a length and width of up to a mile. The straight line winds were estimated to be between 70 and 80 mph.||The damage included the area from just west of the northern tip of Glenerie Boulevard, extending east across the CSX railroad tracks and up a steep hill to North Drive, crossing Route 9W to Old Stage Road.||In addition to minor damage to homes, one mobile home and a few cars were badly damaged. A large tree also reportedly fell onto a freight train as it passed through the area, although there were no reports of injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a southerly flow of warm humid air ahead of an approaching cold front. The late afternoon thunderstorms produced gusty winds estimated around 60 mph across parts of the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. Trees and power lines were downed by the winds. Power outages were reported in Lewiston, Colden, Ransomville and Elma. A significant amount of damage occurred at Fredonia Central Schools in Fredonia. Power poles were snapped. At the ball fields the concrete walls of two dugouts were blown over. The wall and garage door of a concrete block storage building were blown in. Additional damage occurred nearby as several large trees were uprooted and the wind peeled back a section of the metal roof of a building.

A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.

A weak upper level disturbance overriding a ridge over the Northeast United States produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across upstate New York. Several of the storms reached severe levels, with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires were blown down.

The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.

A strong cold front moved from west to east across eastern New York and western New England during Monday afternoon on June 16th. Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed with and preceding the front, many of which were accompanied by large hail.||The widespread and large hail led to significant damage of tree fruits, berries and vegetables across eastern New York, with preliminary damage estimates exceeding $23 million. The worst hit area was within the mid Hudson Valley region, particularly across portions of Ulster and Columbia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in East Greenbush during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a southerly flow of warm humid air ahead of an approaching cold front. The late afternoon thunderstorms produced gusty winds estimated around 60 mph across parts of the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. Trees and power lines were downed by the winds. Power outages were reported in Lewiston, Colden, Ransomville and Elma. A significant amount of damage occurred at Fredonia Central Schools in Fredonia. Power poles were snapped. At the ball fields the concrete walls of two dugouts were blown over. The wall and garage door of a concrete block storage building were blown in. Additional damage occurred nearby as several large trees were uprooted and the wind peeled back a section of the metal roof of a building.

The combination of an approaching cold front from the west, and an unseasonably hot, humid and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during Tuesday afternoon and evening across eastern New York and adjacent western New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team found evidence of damaging straight line winds from a thunderstorm, extending from Malta, across Route 9P on the southeast and east side of Saratoga Lake, into Schuylerville. Numerous downed trees and snapped power lines were found, with estimated peak winds of 85 to 100 mph.||In addition, golf ball size hail was also observed in Schuylerville.||Numerous downed trees resulted in the closure of Route 9P, from Route 76 to the City of Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western and central New Yorkers experienced a rare widespread large and damaging hail event on the 16th of June.  A warm humid airmass at the surface combined with an increasingly cold airmass advecting in aloft. Thunderstorm activity initially focused on the main lake convergence boundary between the Lake Erie and Lake Ontario breezes. The first cells exploded over Niagara and Orleans counties shortly before 1 p.m. and reports of hail came in almost immediately. For the next two hours, cell after cell rolled along a similar path across southern Niagara, southern Orleans, Monroe, Wayne and northern Cayuga counties���many of which had hail of up to an inch-an-a-half diameter. The activity waned for next few hours but after some additional heating due to clearing skies, more hail-filled cells developed. These storms produced hail up to two inches in diameter���a rare event for western New York.  One particular thunderstorm formed over Grand Island, Erie county, and intensified as it moved southeast across the densely-populated northern and eastern suburbs of Buffalo. The golf-ball sized hail damaged thousands of automobiles as well as windows, roofs and awnings on homes. At the Amherst Middle School, the hail pierced 1200 to 1500 holes in the skylight roof. |While property damage was significant, the damage to area crops was devastating. The hailstones pummeled fruits leaving divots and cracks. Vegetable plants were stripped of their leaves. Apples, peaches and pears that were not stripped from the tress and could have been sold for eating fresh will have to be sold for processing at a substantially lower cost because of being misshapen and bruised.|The U.S. Department of Agriculture issued a Disaster Declaration for Erie, Genesee, Monroe, Ontario, Orleans and Wayne counties. Crop damage amounts are estimated since actual dollar losses will not be realized until the agricultural season ends.

A warm humid airmass at the surface combined with an increasingly cold airmass advecting in aloft. Thunderstorm activity initially focused on the main lake convergence boundary between the Lake Erie and Lake Ontario breezes. The first cells exploded over Niagara and Orleans counties shortly before 1 p.m. and reports of hail came in almost immediately. For the next two hours, cell after cell rolled along a similar path across southern Niagara, southern Orleans, Monroe, Wayne and northern Cayuga counties���many of which had hail of up to an inch-an-a-half diameter. The activity waned for next few hours but after some additional heating due to clearing skies, more hail-filled cells developed. These storms produced hail up to two inches in diameter���a rare event for western New York.  One particular thunderstorm formed over Grand Island, Erie county, and intensified as it moved southeast across the densely-populated northern and eastern suburbs of Buffalo. The golf-ball sized hail damaged thousands of automobiles as well as windows, roofs and awnings on homes. At the Amherst Middle School, the hail pierced 1200 to 1500 holes in the skylight roof. |While property damage was significant, the damage to area crops was devastating. The hailstones pummeled fruits leaving divots and cracks. Vegetable plants were stripped of their leaves. Apples, peaches and pears that were not stripped from the tress and could have been sold for eating fresh will have to be sold for processing at a substantially lower cost because of being misshapen and bruised.|The U.S. Department of Agriculture issued a Disaster Declaration for Erie, Genesee, Monroe, Ontario, Orleans and Wayne counties. Crop damage amounts are estimated since actual dollar losses will not be realized until the agricultural season ends.

A very energetic mid-atmospheric disturbance moved across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of June 10th. This developed a surface low along a cold front, which moved across northern New York during the afternoon. These features moved across a very warm, humid and unstable airmass draped across New York that resulted in two rounds of widespread severe thunderstorms.||The first round moved across northern New York during the early afternoon hours and the second round occurred during the early evening.||In northern New York (Clinton, Essex, Franklin and St. Lawrence counties)...hundreds to thousands of trees were damaged, downed or uprooted which caused downed power lines, fires and structural damage to buildings and vehicles. Tens of thousands of customers lost power due to the storms, with some outages that lasted several days. Some communities affected included Potsdam, Parishville and Colton (St. Lawrence county), Moira, Malone and Vermontville (Franklin county), Peru, Plattsburgh and Ellenburg (Clinton county) as well as Wilmington, Westport and Jay (Essex county).||Several fallen trees resulted in injuries and one death resulted from a motorcyclist driving into a downed tree across the road in Wilmington (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed by thunderstorms winds in Wilmington, including Route 86 and Springfield road. Motorcyclist hit fallen tree on road and died from his injuries.

A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.

A weak upper level disturbance overriding a ridge over the Northeast United States produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across upstate New York. Several of the storms reached severe levels, with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on trailers off Route 7. A medium size wooden shed was lifted off its foundation and blown across a field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across central New York in a hot and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front. Many of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage was done at Camp Kiwanis when severe thunderstorm winds hit the area. Several large trees fell at the center of the camp, destroying the fire circle, dumpster, canoes, fencing and some program equipment. Damage to buildings was minimal, with some roof repairs needed.|A National Weather Service storm survey team determined the damage was caused by straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across central New York in a hot and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front. Many of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.

A pre-frontal trough well ahead of a cold front moved into a sultry airmass with ample instability and wind shear. A weak supercell formed near Erie, Pennsylvania and grew as it encountered an outflow from earlier convection. The storm continued to intensify as it moved across the western southern tier of New York and reach severe limits as it cross the eastern Finger Lakes and eastern Lake Ontario Regions of New York. The thunderstorms produced hail up to three-quarter inch in diameter and wind gusts were estimated to near 70 mph. The storms cut power to nearly 75,000 customers for several hours through the evening. Falling trees damaged homes and automobiles in Avon and York, Livingston county and Phoenix, Oswego and Sandy Pond in Oswego county. Oswego County was particularly hard hit. In Sandy Creek, between 500 and 600 trees were downed at the Elms Golf Club. The utilities reported about 100 power poles downed by the storms. Two funnels were spotted���one in Cayuga County and one in Jefferson County however no touchdown occurred in either case. One death occurred with the storms. In the town of Croghan, a 47-year-old logger was crushed by a falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and a few thunderstorms developed out ahead of a cold frontal boundary. There were isolated severe thunderstorms east of Lake Ontario, and one of these storms produced a weak tornado.

A pre-frontal trough well ahead of a cold front moved into a sultry airmass with ample instability and wind shear. A weak supercell formed near Erie, Pennsylvania and grew as it encountered an outflow from earlier convection. The storm continued to intensify as it moved across the western southern tier of New York and reach severe limits as it cross the eastern Finger Lakes and eastern Lake Ontario Regions of New York. The thunderstorms produced hail up to three-quarter inch in diameter and wind gusts were estimated to near 70 mph. The storms cut power to nearly 75,000 customers for several hours through the evening. Falling trees damaged homes and automobiles in Avon and York, Livingston county and Phoenix, Oswego and Sandy Pond in Oswego county. Oswego County was particularly hard hit. In Sandy Creek, between 500 and 600 trees were downed at the Elms Golf Club. The utilities reported about 100 power poles downed by the storms. Two funnels were spotted���one in Cayuga County and one in Jefferson County however no touchdown occurred in either case. One death occurred with the storms. In the town of Croghan, a 47-year-old logger was crushed by a falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very energetic mid-atmospheric disturbance moved across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of June 10th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western and central New Yorkers experienced a rare widespread large and damaging hail event on the 16th of June.  A warm humid airmass at the surface combined with an increasingly cold airmass advecting in aloft. Thunderstorm activity initially focused on the main lake convergence boundary between the Lake Erie and Lake Ontario breezes. The first cells exploded over Niagara and Orleans counties shortly before 1 p.m. and reports of hail came in almost immediately. For the next two hours, cell after cell rolled along a similar path across southern Niagara, southern Orleans, Monroe, Wayne and northern Cayuga counties���many of which had hail of up to an inch-an-a-half diameter. The activity waned for next few hours but after some additional heating due to clearing skies, more hail-filled cells developed. These storms produced hail up to two inches in diameter���a rare event for western New York.  One particular thunderstorm formed over Grand Island, Erie county, and intensified as it moved southeast across the densely-populated northern and eastern suburbs of Buffalo. The golf-ball sized hail damaged thousands of automobiles as well as windows, roofs and awnings on homes. At the Amherst Middle School, the hail pierced 1200 to 1500 holes in the skylight roof. |While property damage was significant, the damage to area crops was devastating. The hailstones pummeled fruits leaving divots and cracks. Vegetable plants were stripped of their leaves. Apples, peaches and pears that were not stripped from the tress and could have been sold for eating fresh will have to be sold for processing at a substantially lower cost because of being misshapen and bruised.|The U.S. Department of Agriculture issued a Disaster Declaration for Erie, Genesee, Monroe, Ontario, Orleans and Wayne counties. Crop damage amounts are estimated since actual dollar losses will not be realized until the agricultural season ends.

An approaching cold front across central New York state triggered numerous severe thunderstorms across the region. Damaging winds and large hail impacted many areas, from the interior to the coast. In addition to the wind and hail, a tornado produced damage in and near Newburgh, New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down three times along a mile and a half path in Newburgh, NY. The tornado developed around 615 pm and moved east-southeast from the 400 block of South Plank Road to Park Avenue between 2nd and 3rd Streets. The tornado brought down multiple large branches and a few large trees. It also caused a wall to collapse at Rick's Automotive on South Plank Road, causing the roof to collapse. The damage was consistent with an EF0 tornado on the Enhanced Fujita Scale, with winds from 65 to 85 MPH.

Western and central New Yorkers experienced a rare widespread large and damaging hail event on the 16th of June.  A warm humid airmass at the surface combined with an increasingly cold airmass advecting in aloft. For the next two hours, cell after cell rolled along a similar path across southern Niagara, southern Orleans, Monroe, Wayne and northern Cayuga counties���many of which had hail of up to an inch-an-a-half diameter. The activity waned for next few hours but after some additional heating due to clearing skies, more hail-filled cells developed. These storms produced hail up to two inches in diameter���a rare event for western New York. The hailstones pummeled fruits leaving divots and cracks. Vegetable plants were stripped of their leaves. Crop damage amounts are estimated since actual dollar losses will not be realized until the agricultural season ends.

A frontal boundary retreated to the north of the region during Friday, July 18th, allowing hot and humid conditions to overspread eastern New York and adjacent western New England. This contributed to the development of numerous thunderstorms during Friday afternoon and evening, some of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, as well as large tree limbs were reported down in several locations within the City of Albany due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid and unstable air mass contributed to the development of isolated severe thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York during Saturday afternoon on July 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, along with wires were reported down in East Schodack due to strong thunderstorm winds.||Several trees were reported down on Hy Drive, another tree fell on Hillview Street, and another large tree snapped and fell across power lines at the intersection of Brookside Drive and Route 150.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western and central New Yorkers experienced a rare widespread large and damaging hail event on the 16th of June.  A warm humid airmass at the surface combined with an increasingly cold airmass advecting in aloft. Thunderstorm activity initially focused on the main lake convergence boundary between the Lake Erie and Lake Ontario breezes. The first cells exploded over Niagara and Orleans counties shortly before 1 p.m. and reports of hail came in almost immediately. For the next two hours, cell after cell rolled along a similar path across southern Niagara, southern Orleans, Monroe, Wayne and northern Cayuga counties���many of which had hail of up to an inch-an-a-half diameter. The activity waned for next few hours but after some additional heating due to clearing skies, more hail-filled cells developed. These storms produced hail up to two inches in diameter���a rare event for western New York.  One particular thunderstorm formed over Grand Island, Erie county, and intensified as it moved southeast across the densely-populated northern and eastern suburbs of Buffalo. The golf-ball sized hail damaged thousands of automobiles as well as windows, roofs and awnings on homes. At the Amherst Middle School, the hail pierced 1200 to 1500 holes in the skylight roof. |While property damage was significant, the damage to area crops was devastating. The hailstones pummeled fruits leaving divots and cracks. Vegetable plants were stripped of their leaves. Apples, peaches and pears that were not stripped from the tress and could have been sold for eating fresh will have to be sold for processing at a substantially lower cost because of being misshapen and bruised.|The U.S. Department of Agriculture issued a Disaster Declaration for Erie, Genesee, Monroe, Ontario, Orleans and Wayne counties. Crop damage amounts are estimated since actual dollar losses will not be realized until the agricultural season ends.

The combination of an approaching cold front from the west, and an unseasonably hot, humid and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during Tuesday afternoon and evening across eastern New York and adjacent western New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down near Lake Pleasant due to strong thunderstorm winds. Some of the trees were blocking roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain which caused localized Flash Flooding in the Bronx.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain caused the intersection of E. 193rd St. and Webster Ave. to be impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing upper level trough, combined with several weak surface boundaries, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms during Sunday afternoon and evening on June 22nd, some of which became severe.||Additional isolated severe thunderstorms developed late Sunday night, into the early morning hours of Monday June 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in multiple locations in and near Clifton Park due to strong thunderstorm winds. Some of the reports included trees and wires down near the junction of Routes 146 and 146A in Jonesville, at the intersection of Kinns Road and Twilight Drive, and on Bruno Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cold front approaching from the west, and a warm and very moist air mass in place, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across the mid Hudson Valley of eastern New York during the afternoon hours of Saturday June 14th. Some of the thunderstorms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three hikers and a baby were slightly injured by a nearby lightning strike while sitting on a bench at the Mohonk Mountain House near New Paltz. The group were walking on a trail when the thunderstorm started. They then took cover and sat on a bench when lightning struck nearby. Their feet were in water, exacerbating the impact of the nearby lightning strike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 149 near Dodge Hill Road in Granville was closed due to flash flooding during a thunderstorm.

A strong cold front moved from west to east across eastern New York and western New England during Monday afternoon on June 16th. Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed with and preceding the front, many of which were accompanied by large hail.||The widespread and large hail led to significant damage of tree fruits, berries and vegetables across eastern New York, with preliminary damage estimates exceeding $23 million. The worst hit area was within the mid Hudson Valley region, particularly across portions of Ulster and Columbia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Ashland during a thunderstorm.||The large hail damaged tree fruits, berries and vegetables near Ashland, and across other areas within Greene County. Total preliminary crop damage estimates from the hail in Greene County were approximately $169 thousand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Southeast New York the evening of Tuesday June 10th causing multiple severe thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree fell onto a house on Cedar Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front across central New York state triggered numerous severe thunderstorms across the region. Damaging winds and large hail impacted many areas, from the interior to the coast. In addition to the wind and hail, a tornado produced damage in and near Newburgh, New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed, blocking Dayton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-atmospheric disturbance moved into the eastern Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River Valley of New York during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 26th. This caused the development of scattered thunderstorms across St. Lawrence county which produced localized wind damage in Gouverneur, Macomb and Morristown vicinity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines reported downed by thunderstorm winds in the Morristown - Brier Hill vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-atmospheric disturbance moved into the eastern Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River Valley of New York during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 26th. This caused the development of scattered thunderstorms across St. Lawrence county which produced localized wind damage in Gouverneur, Macomb and Morristown vicinity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported downed by thunderstorm winds in the town of Macomb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-atmospheric disturbance moved into the eastern Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River Valley of New York during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 26th. This caused the development of scattered thunderstorms across St. Lawrence county which produced localized wind damage in Gouverneur, Macomb and Morristown vicinity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported downed by thunderstorm winds in Gouverneur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very energetic mid-atmospheric disturbance moved across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of June 10th. This developed a surface low along a cold front, which moved across northern New York during the afternoon. These features moved across a very warm, humid and unstable airmass draped across New York that resulted in two rounds of widespread severe thunderstorms.||The first round moved across northern New York during the early afternoon hours and the second round occurred during the early evening.||In northern New York (Clinton, Essex, Franklin and St. Lawrence counties)...hundreds to thousands of trees were damaged, downed or uprooted which caused downed power lines, fires and structural damage to buildings and vehicles. Tens of thousands of customers lost power due to the storms, with some outages that lasted several days. Some communities affected included Potsdam, Parishville and Colton (St. Lawrence county), Moira, Malone and Vermontville (Franklin county), Peru, Plattsburgh and Ellenburg (Clinton county) as well as Wilmington, Westport and Jay (Essex county).||Several fallen trees resulted in injuries and one death resulted from a motorcyclist driving into a downed tree across the road in Wilmington (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm winds in Beekmantown, north Plattsburgh and Chazy area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very energetic mid-atmospheric disturbance moved across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of June 10th. This developed a surface low along a cold front, which moved across northern New York during the afternoon. These features moved across a very warm, humid and unstable airmass draped across New York that resulted in two rounds of widespread severe thunderstorms.||The first round moved across northern New York during the early afternoon hours and the second round occurred during the early evening.||In northern New York (Clinton, Essex, Franklin and St. Lawrence counties)...hundreds to thousands of trees were damaged, downed or uprooted which caused downed power lines, fires and structural damage to buildings and vehicles. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees snapped or downed by thunderstorm winds, along with power lines that have blocked several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across central New York in a hot and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front. Many of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorm winds knocked down swaths of trees which included three dozen large trees snapped off or uprooted in the hamlet of Smallwood. A number of homes suffered extensive damage when large trees fell on them. NYSEG reported 12,000 customers without power at the peak of the storm. Winds were estimated to be 60 to 80 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the upper atmosphere brought showers and scattered thunderstorms to the area. Isolated storms produced hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were broken off and wires were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.

The golf-ball sized hail damaged thousands of automobiles as well as windows, roofs and awnings on homes. At the Amherst Middle School, the hail pierced 1200 to 1500 holes in the skylight roof. |While property damage was significant, the damage to area crops was devastating. The hailstones pummeled fruits leaving divots and cracks. Vegetable plants were stripped of their leaves. Apples, peaches and pears that were not stripped from the tress and could have been sold for eating fresh will have to be sold for processing at a substantially lower cost because of being misshapen and bruised.|The U.S. Department of Agriculture issued a Disaster Declaration for Erie, Genesee, Monroe, Ontario, Orleans and Wayne counties. Crop damage amounts are estimated since actual dollar losses will not be realized until the agricultural season ends.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western and central New Yorkers experienced a rare widespread large and damaging hail event on the 16th of June.  A warm humid airmass at the surface combined with an increasingly cold airmass advecting in aloft. Thunderstorm activity initially focused on the main lake convergence boundary between the Lake Erie and Lake Ontario breezes. The first cells exploded over Niagara and Orleans counties shortly before 1 p.m. and reports of hail came in almost immediately. For the next two hours, cell after cell rolled along a similar path across southern Niagara, southern Orleans, Monroe, Wayne and northern Cayuga counties���many of which had hail of up to an inch-an-a-half diameter. The activity waned for next few hours but after some additional heating due to clearing skies, more hail-filled cells developed. These storms produced hail up to two inches in diameter���a rare event for western New York.  One particular thunderstorm formed over Grand Island, Erie county, and intensified as it moved southeast across the densely-populated northern and eastern suburbs of Buffalo. The golf-ball sized hail damaged thousands of automobiles as well as windows, roofs and awnings on homes. At the Amherst Middle School, the hail pierced 1200 to 1500 holes in the skylight roof. |While property damage was significant, the damage to area crops was devastating. The hailstones pummeled fruits leaving divots and cracks. Vegetable plants were stripped of their leaves. Apples, peaches and pears that were not stripped from the tress and could have been sold for eating fresh will have to be sold for processing at a substantially lower cost because of being misshapen and bruised.|The U.S. Department of Agriculture issued a Disaster Declaration for Erie, Genesee, Monroe, Ontario, Orleans and Wayne counties. Crop damage amounts are estimated since actual dollar losses will not be realized until the agricultural season ends.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very energetic mid-atmospheric disturbance moved across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of June 10th. This developed a surface low along a cold front, which moved across northern New York during the afternoon. These features moved across a very warm, humid and unstable airmass draped across New York that resulted in two rounds of widespread severe thunderstorms.||The first round moved across northern New York during the early afternoon hours and the second round occurred during the early evening.||In northern New York (Clinton, Essex, Franklin and St. Lawrence counties)...hundreds to thousands of trees were damaged, downed or uprooted which caused downed power lines, fires and structural damage to buildings and vehicles. Tens of thousands of customers lost power due to the storms, with some outages that lasted several days. Some communities affected included Potsdam, Parishville and Colton (St. Lawrence county), Moira, Malone and Vermontville (Franklin county), Peru, Plattsburgh and Ellenburg (Clinton county) as well as Wilmington, Westport and Jay (Essex county).||Several fallen trees resulted in injuries and one death resulted from a motorcyclist driving into a downed tree across the road in Wilmington (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down across Altona.

A very energetic mid-atmospheric disturbance moved across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of June 10th. This developed a surface low along a cold front, which moved across northern New York during the afternoon. These features moved across a very warm, humid and unstable airmass draped across New York that resulted in two rounds of widespread severe thunderstorms.||The first round moved across northern New York during the early afternoon hours and the second round occurred during the early evening.||In northern New York (Clinton, Essex, Franklin and St. Lawrence counties)...hundreds to thousands of trees were damaged, downed or uprooted which caused downed power lines, fires and structural damage to buildings and vehicles. Tens of thousands of customers lost power due to the storms, with some outages that lasted several days. Some communities affected included Potsdam, Parishville and Colton (St. Lawrence county), Moira, Malone and Vermontville (Franklin county), Peru, Plattsburgh and Ellenburg (Clinton county) as well as Wilmington, Westport and Jay (Essex county).||Several fallen trees resulted in injuries and one death resulted from a motorcyclist driving into a downed tree across the road in Wilmington (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down across Saranac Lake vicinity along Route 3 between|Saranac Village and Tupper Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary retreated to the north of the region during Friday, July 18th, allowing hot and humid conditions to overspread eastern New York and adjacent western New England. This contributed to the development of numerous thunderstorms during Friday afternoon and evening, some of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two girls were struck by lightning near a main gate at the Saratoga County Fairgrounds in Ballston Spa.  One girl, age 15, was rushed to the intensive care unit at Albany Medical Center, while the other female, age 34, was rushed to Saratoga Hospital.||The two girls were standing near a main gate at the Fairgrounds when lightning struck a nearby tree during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front extending from southern New England through the Delmarva region pulled eastward as low pressure developed along the boundary. As moisture pulled northward, heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed, producing severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of around 3.5 inches was measured from thunderstorms. National Weather Service doppler radar estimated that as much as 5 inches of rain fell in various small basins in the county during a three hour time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several mid-atmospheric impulses traveled along a stationary boundary across northern New York during the early afternoon and evening hours of July 18th. This stationary boundary separated warm, humid air across much of New York from cooler, drier air across the international border with Canada.||The first round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon along the Canadian-northern New York border in northern Franklin county and moved across Clinton county toward Vermont. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines between Chateaugay (Franklin county) and Chazy (Clinton county).||A second round of severe thunderstorms moved across St. Lawrence and northern Franklin counties during the evening. Numerous reports of downed trees causing downed power lines and some structural damage in the Norfolk, Norwood region as well as affecting the Malone (Franklin county) area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm winds in the Moria, Brushton, Bangor and Malone area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across eastern New York and western New England during the late morning through mid afternoon hours of Sunday July 27th. The interaction of this front, and a warm, moist and unstable air mass, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across the eastern Catskills, mid Hudson Valley region, and the central and southern Taconics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Survey Team determined that straight line wind damage occurred in portions of Cairo, South Cairo, and Leeds during the early afternoon hours of Sunday July 27th.||Fifty to one hundred pine trees were sheared off along County Route 67 in South Cairo around 1135 LST, with peak winds estimated at 100 mph.||In addition, several trees were uprooted on Rocky Road near South Cairo around 1135 LST, some of which fell through homes. Two homes were badly damaged. Peak winds were estimated between 100 and 110 mph.||Numerous trees were also downed in a fan like pattern on Mclaren Road in South Cairo around 1143 LST. Peak estimated winds were 115 to 125 mph.||Farm equipment was also overturned on Pine Farm, located on Pine Lane in Cairo around 1149 LST. Peak estimated winds were 115 to 125 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure rode along a slow moving cold front approaching the area on July 23. A tropical airmass was already in place across the local area from Tropical Storm Cristobal which passed by to our east the previous day. The combination of these systems interacting with this airmass led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 218 was closed in both directions between West Point Academy and the Cornwall line due to flooding. A National Weather Service Cooperative Observer reported a storm total rainfall amount of 2.00 inches in West Point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of standing water accumulated on Old Mill Road at the railroad underpass in Wantagh. NWS mesonets reported storm total rainfall amounts of 3.03 inches in Bellmore, 1.77 inches in Massapequa Park, 1.75 inches in North Bellmore, and 1.57 inches in Merrick.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused significant flash flooding. All roads in the towns of Colchester were closed, and a State of Emergency was declared. 15-20 residents were evacuated near County Highway 7. Serious damage occurred to a bridge on the highway. Portions of the road on Holiday and Berry Brook were completely washed out, and one rescue took place from a car. Flooding also occurred along Route 206 from Rockland to Route 30. Highway 17 west of Roscoe, Old Route 17 near Roscoe and Route 17 eastbound east of Roscoe were all closed due to washouts of portions of the highway. Water surrounded a home in Jeffersonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area on July 27 which produced thunderstorms with torrential downpours and led to flash flooding across Long Island and portions of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floyd Road in Shirley was impassable due to flooding. A storm total rainfall report of 3.87 inches was received by the public in Shirley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along a stationary front south of Long Island and produced heavy rain across portions of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saw Mill River Parkway was closed northbound between exits 27 (Marble Ave.) and 33 (Roaring Brook Rd.) and southbound between exits 32 |(RT-120) and 30 (Grant St.) due to flooding. A mesonet in the town of Chappaqua and an NWS co-op observer in Yorktown Heights recorded storm total rainfall amounts of 4.33 inches and 1.85 inches respectively. A few other mesonet sites immediately surrounding Chappaqua recorded between 2 and 3 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out to the northeast of the region, pulling a cold front into New England. Meanwhile, low pressure in the upper atmosphere kept unsettled weather across upstate New York. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed, producing a few reports of flooding and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 2.14 inches of rain in 24 hours ending at 7 a.m., then another 2 inches from 7 a.m. to 930 a.m. Parts of a roadway were washed away below this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several mid-atmospheric impulses traveled along a stationary boundary across northern New York during the early afternoon and evening hours of July 18th. This stationary boundary separated warm, humid air across much of New York from cooler, drier air across the international border with Canada.||The first round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon along the Canadian-northern New York border in northern Franklin county and moved across Clinton county toward Vermont. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines between Chateaugay (Franklin county) and Chazy (Clinton county).||A second round of severe thunderstorms moved across St. Lawrence and northern Franklin counties during the evening. Numerous reports of downed trees causing downed power lines and some structural damage in the Norfolk, Norwood region as well as affecting the Malone (Franklin county) area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines down across Norwood, Norfolk and North Stockholm. Several large 100-150 year old maple and oak trees fallen onto vehicles, homes and other structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescues were performed at the intersection of Merritts Rd. and Hempstead Turpike in Farmingdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front extending from southern New England through the Delmarva region pulled eastward as low pressure developed along the boundary. As moisture pulled northward, heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed, producing severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gas station roof was partially blown off. A garbage dumpster, vehicle, and other small objects were blown through the air. Hundred of trees were blown down. A damage survey team determined that the damage was caused by downburst, straight-line winds with speeds around 70 mph, and peak gusts of 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak low pressure system moving east out of eastern Ontario. Some of the storms reached severe levels, producing large hail and damaging winds.

A slow moving cold front moved from the central Great Lakes to Western New York by early afternoon. With sufficient sunshine ahead of the boundary, temperatures warmed into the 80s, with increasing moisture. This unstable air mass caused scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail, and some isolated wind damage reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong upper level disturbance, combined with a moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York State during Thursday afternoon on August 7th, some of which contained large hail. In addition, very heavy rainfall resulted in significant urban flash flooding in and near downtown Albany.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Hudson Falls during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through a moderately unstable air mass across northern New York during the late afternoon and early evening hours of August 18th. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms accompanied this cold front, especially in St. Lawrence county with several reports of damaging winds. Other damaging wind reports associated with these storms occurred in Franklin and Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed on power lines along Ryan road in Saranac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low center that was the remnants of Hurricane Ike reached the lower Great Lakes region during the late evening of the 14th and brought high winds to the area as it lifted from Indiana across southern Ontario. Wind gusts were measured to 66 mph. The winds downed trees and power lines throughout the area. The wet soil conditions contributed to the number of downed trees. Debris blocked many streets and highways. There were numerous reports of homes, building and automobiles being damaged by fallen trees. In Seneca Point, winds ripped the roof off a barn. Several school districts were forced to cancel classes on the 15th out of safety for the students. In Adams Center, about half of the pool roof at Clarke Senior High School blew off. A bumper crop of late season corn was damaged. It was estimated in Jefferson and Lewis Counties that nearly 70% of the crop was flattened. Marine interests were not spared the wrath of the storm. In Buffalo, three floating docks were ripped from their concrete footings and tilted making them inaccessible to several hundred slip holders. Two boats were capsized off Buffalo. In Chaumont Bay, Jefferson County, several boats were grounded when a dock broke adrift. The storm surge was estimated between 10 and 12 feet at the east end of Lake Ontario.  In Oswego Harbor, two sailboats were sunk. Damages were estimated due to the widespread nature of the event. Specific measured wind gusts included: 66 m.p.h. at Oswego, 59 m.p.h. at Niagara Falls and Attica, 55 m.p.h. at Pembroke and Victor, 54 m.p.h. at Albion, 53 m.p.h. at Kendall, Brockport, Geneseo, and Canandaigua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ike moved northeast from the Ohio Valley through the eastern Great Lakes overnight from the 14th to the 15th. This storm brought high winds from the Finger Lakes to the eastern Lake Ontario region of north central New York.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 50 to 60 mph. Damage occurred to trees and power lines, as many power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were blown down in the city of Auburn.

An upper level low pressure system moved slowly northeast from central Pennsylvania into east central New York State during Monday, August 11th. The upper level dynamics of this system, combined with abundant low level moisture, and significant instability led to the development of numerous, slow moving thunderstorm clusters during Monday afternoon. Large hail was reported with many of these thunderstorms.||In addition, locally very heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding in portions of eastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed in various portions of Columbia County due to flash flooding from very heavy rainfall.||In Hillsdale, Rodman, Wolf Hill, and Pumpkin Roads were closed due to significant flooding. A high volume of water, seven feet deep and backed up over a 20 acre swamp, was believed to have burst from a beaver dam, sending a cascade of water down Wolf Hill Road.||In addition, School Street in Chatham was also closed due to flooding.

Large hail was reported with many of these thunderstorms.||In addition, locally very heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding in portions of eastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted from very heavy rainfall in Middleville. Flooding was reported at the intersection of State Routes 169 and 29 at the intersection of Route 28 in Middleville.  Some of this flooding was attributed to nearby Bridenbecker Creek flowing out of its banks.||In addition, three homes on South Main Street, and two on Bridge Street experienced basement flooding.

In Chaumont Bay, Jefferson County, several boats were grounded when a dock broke adrift. The storm surge was estimated between 10 and 12 feet at the east end of Lake Ontario.  In Oswego Harbor, two sailboats were sunk. Damages were estimated due to the widespread nature of the event. Specific measured wind gusts included: 66 m.p.h. at Oswego, 59 m.p.h. at Niagara Falls and Attica, 55 m.p.h. at Pembroke and Victor, 54 m.p.h. at Albion, 53 m.p.h. at Kendall, Brockport, Geneseo, and Canandaigua.

The low center that was the remnants of Hurricane Ike reached the lower Great Lakes region during the late evening of the 14th and brought high winds to the area as it lifted from Indiana across southern Ontario. Wind gusts were measured to 66 mph. The winds downed trees and power lines throughout the area. The wet soil conditions contributed to the number of downed trees. Debris blocked many streets and highways. There were numerous reports of homes, building and automobiles being damaged by fallen trees. In Seneca Point, winds ripped the roof off a barn. Several school districts were forced to cancel classes on the 15th out of safety for the students. In Adams Center, about half of the pool roof at Clarke Senior High School blew off. A wave of low pressure moving along a stationary front located over the Tri-State triggered some severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley and Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on power lines along Head Of Pond Road in Watermill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomolous slow moving upper level low pressure system in combination with a weak surface cold front and local seabreezes triggered thunderstorms producing torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding on Long Island and in portions of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hutchinson River Pkwy. was closed in both directions near Lincoln Ave. in Pelham due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moving along a stationary front draped across the local area produced thunderstorms with torrential downpours leading to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many of the secondary roads in the town of Southampton were impassable due to flooding. Windmill Lane was covered by approximately 2 ft. of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly northeast from central Pennsylvania into east central New York State during Monday, August 11th. The upper level dynamics of this system, combined with abundant low level moisture, and significant instability led to the development of numerous, slow moving thunderstorm clusters during Monday afternoon. Large hail was reported with many of these thunderstorms.||In addition, locally very heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding in portions of eastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall from training thunderstorms led to significant urban and small stream flash flooding in and near the City of Rensselaer. A State of Emergency was declared in the City of Rensselaer as numerous roads were closed, with 6 or more feet of standing water reported on city streets. Evacuations of 50 to 75 homes occurred due to the flooding. Flooding was mainly concentrated along and near the banks of Quackenberry Creek, as well as near Mill Creek.||The Amtrak Station in Rensselaer was also closed due to the flooding.||Flash flooding also closed Routes 9 and 20, a portion of Route 151, and Luther Road, in East Greenbush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system along a nearly stationary boundary intensified as it tracked northeast from western Pennsylvania into central New York from Wednesday morning on July 23rd, through Thursday morning on July 24th. A cold front associated with this low slowly moved east across eastern New York late Wednesday afternoon into early Thursday morning.||The combination of strong upper level dynamics, and abundant moisture in place, led to widespread showers and thunderstorms containing heavy rainfall. This led to flooding across portions of eastern New York from Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in the closure of eastbound lanes of the New York State Thruway, Interstate 90, at mile marker 169, with at least 2 to 3 feet of water on the road. In addition, the westbound lanes were accumulating significant amounts of water as well.||Numerous roads were washed out across the county. In the town of Amsterdam, some of the flooded roads which sustained damage included Truax Road, Cranes Hollow Road, Chapman Drive, Widow Susan Road, and Swarts Hill Road. In addition, properties along the South Chuctanunda Creek near Amsterdam also experienced flooding.||In the town of Florida, Peck and Mckinney Roads were closed due to washed out culverts, with some bridges damaged and needing further inspection, including a bridge on Hartley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system along a nearly stationary boundary intensified as it tracked northeast from western Pennsylvania into central New York from Wednesday morning on July 23rd, through Thursday morning on July 24th. A cold front associated with this low slowly moved east across eastern New York late Wednesday afternoon into early Thursday morning.||The combination of strong upper level dynamics, and abundant moisture in place, led to widespread showers and thunderstorms containing heavy rainfall. This led to flooding across portions of eastern New York from Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed across Albany County due to flash flooding. Some of these roads included Beebe Road in Knox, the intersection of Fuller Road and Western Avenue in Guilderland, the intersection of Western Avenue and Schoolhouse Road in Guilderland, and also Route 5 between Osborne Road and the Interstate 90 overpass.||In addition, significant damage occurred to a home in McKownville, near Guilderland, where a foundation wall collapsed, allowing water and mud to enter the basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved across the area producing thunderstorms with torrential rainfall causing flash flooding across portions of Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorist became stranded in high water on Deer Field Rd. in Water Mill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east across northern New England during the morning hours of Wednesday August 6th. An upper level disturbance in the wake of this system, combined with a moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of isolated severe thunderstorms across portions of the upper Hudson Valley early Wednesday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell during a thunderstorm in Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly northeast from central Pennsylvania into east central New York State during Monday, August 11th. The upper level dynamics of this system, combined with abundant low level moisture, and significant instability led to the development of numerous, slow moving thunderstorm clusters during Monday afternoon. Large hail was reported with many of these thunderstorms.||In addition, locally very heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding in portions of eastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused water to overflow on East Hills Road in Loudonville, approximately 300 yards south of Route 155.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong upper level disturbance, combined with a moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York State during Thursday afternoon on August 7th, some of which contained large hail. In addition, very heavy rainfall resulted in significant urban flash flooding in and near downtown Albany.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to significant urban flooding in downtown Albany late Thursday afternoon. One to four inches of rainfall occurred within two hours across a small portion of downtown. This overwhelmed the storm drainage system within the city, flooding several streets. Hackett Boulevard and Northern Boulevard had water deep enough to submerge vehicles, with several cars swept away outside of the Hackett Dale apartments. In addition, water burst through a manhole on South Pearl Street near Arch Street, shooting water at least two stories high. Flooding also closed Holland Avenue, and water at least 8 inches deep was pouring over the curb on Broadway, near the State Department of Environmental Conservation building.||Numerous basements were also flooded within the city of Albany, as well as in portions of Guilderland, Delmar and Bethlehem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly northeast from central Pennsylvania into east central New York State during Monday, August 11th. The upper level dynamics of this system, combined with abundant low level moisture, and significant instability led to the development of numerous, slow moving thunderstorm clusters during Monday afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed across Montgomery County due to flash flooding from very heavy rainfall. Some of the roads that were closed included, but were not limited to, Logtown Road and Van Epps Road near Glen.||In addition, a minor mud slide occurred at the intersection of Route 5 and Switzer Hill Road just east of Fonda, after a culvert underneath Route 5 was inundated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roadways remained closed across portions of Saratoga County from earlier heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low center that was the remnants of Hurricane Ike reached the lower Great Lakes region during the late evening of the 14th and brought high winds to the area as it lifted from Indiana across southern Ontario. Wind gusts were measured to 66 mph. The winds downed trees and power lines throughout the area. The wet soil conditions contributed to the number of downed trees. Debris blocked many streets and highways. There were numerous reports of homes, building and automobiles being damaged by fallen trees. In Seneca Point, winds ripped the roof off a barn. Several school districts were forced to cancel classes on the 15th out of safety for the students. In Adams Center, about half of the pool roof at Clarke Senior High School blew off. A bumper crop of late season corn was damaged. It was estimated in Jefferson and Lewis Counties that nearly 70% of the crop was flattened. Marine interests were not spared the wrath of the storm. In Buffalo, three floating docks were ripped from their concrete footings and tilted making them inaccessible to several hundred slip holders. Two boats were capsized off Buffalo. In Chaumont Bay, Jefferson County, several boats were grounded when a dock broke adrift. The storm surge was estimated between 10 and 12 feet at the east end of Lake Ontario.  In Oswego Harbor, two sailboats were sunk. Damages were estimated due to the widespread nature of the event. Specific measured wind gusts included: 66 m.p.h. at Oswego, 59 m.p.h. at Niagara Falls and Attica, 55 m.p.h. at Pembroke and Victor, 54 m.p.h. at Albion, 53 m.p.h. at Kendall, Brockport, Geneseo, and Canandaigua.

A slow moving cold front moved from the central Great Lakes to Western New York by early afternoon. With sufficient sunshine ahead of the boundary, temperatures warmed into the 80s, with increasing moisture. This unstable air mass caused scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail, and some isolated wind damage reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a stationary front parked south of Long Island, an unstable airmass produced thunderstorms in the New York City metropolitan area. Lightning struck a building in Bay Ridge and caused structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four story brick residence was struck by lightning, causing damage to portions of the roof. A two by three foot section of the brick roof was dislodged and fell to the street and sidewalk below. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system along a nearly stationary boundary intensified as it tracked northeast from western Pennsylvania into central New York from Wednesday morning on July 23rd, through Thursday morning on July 24th. A cold front associated with this low slowly moved east across eastern New York late Wednesday afternoon into early Thursday morning.||The combination of strong upper level dynamics, and abundant moisture in place, led to widespread showers and thunderstorms containing heavy rainfall. This led to flooding across portions of eastern New York from Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed across Schoharie County due to flash flooding. These included, but were not limited to, Creek Road to Esperance and to the Montgomery County line, Treadlemire Road to Middleburgh, Junction Road, Zicha Road, Sanatarium Road to Esperance and Schoharie, Benninger Road, Barton Hill Road, Sheldon Road, and portions of Route 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the upper atmosphere continued to be the focus for scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. Some of the storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and largely amplified upper atmospheric trough across the Great Lakes and east coast of the United States developed a strong, cold autumnal storm along a cold frontal boundary late on October 27th into the early hours of October 28th. This storm strengthened rapidly as it traveled north along a cold front across central New England on the 28th.||Sub-freezing air across northern New York, especially away from Lake Champlain accounted for a significant accumulation of heavy, wet snow across the Adirondacks and St. Lawrence River Valley. Snowfall accumulations of more than a foot occurred across much of the Adirondacks with 6 to 12 inches within the St. Lawrence River Valley.||Some specific snowfall accumulations included; 18 inches in Wanakena (St. Lawrence county), 16 inches in Tupper Lake (Franklin county), 15 inches in Lake Placid (Essex county) and 14 inches in Saranac Lake (Franklin county).||Significant accumulations of heavy wet snowfall accounted for scattered tree damage and power outages as well as numerous vehicle accidents across the region.

High pressure brought clear skies, calm winds, and cold air to the region. Temperatures over the Eastern Lake Ontario Region and interior sections of the Western Southern Tier fell below 32 degrees resulting in a widespread freeze over those areas. Specific readings included: 24 in Andover; 25 in Ellicottville; 27 in Franklinville; 28 in Wellsville and Parish; 29 in Warsaw, Attica, Watertown and Harrisburg; 30 in Mexico and Philadelphia and 31 in Turin.

A low pressure system developed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary just off the northern mid Atlantic coast late Monday night on October 27th, and then intensified rapidly, reaching eastern Long Island by Tuesday morning on October 28th. The low continued to intensify, and lifted north northwest, reaching southeast Quebec by early Wednesday morning on October 29th.||As cold air aloft filtered into this developing storm system, rain changed to heavy wet snow across the higher elevations of the eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, Schoharie Valley, western Mohawk Valley and southwest Adirondacks during Tuesday, before tapering off by early Wednesday morning.||Snowfall amounts of 12 to 20 inches fell across the higher elevations, which led to numerous downed trees, tree limbs and power lines due to the weight of heavy, wet snow. Widespread power outages resulted.||In valley areas, mainly rain fell. However, some wet snow mixed in Tuesday afternoon and evening, producing minor accumulations of 1 to 4 inches in some areas.

This wet, heavy snowfall, combined with gusty winds, led to some downed tree limbs and resultant scattered power outages across the region.

Widespread power outages resulted.||In valley areas, mainly rain fell. However, some wet snow mixed in Tuesday afternoon and evening, producing minor accumulations of 1 to 4 inches in some areas.

A vigorous and largely amplified upper atmospheric trough across the Great Lakes and east coast of the United States developed a strong, cold autumnal storm along a cold frontal boundary late on October 27th into the early hours of October 28th. This storm strengthened rapidly as it traveled north along a cold front across central New England on the 28th.||Sub-freezing air across northern New York, especially away from Lake Champlain accounted for a significant accumulation of heavy, wet snow across the Adirondacks and St. Lawrence River Valley. Snowfall accumulations of more than a foot occurred across much of the Adirondacks with 6 to 12 inches within the St. Lawrence River Valley.||Some specific snowfall accumulations included; 18 inches in Wanakena (St. Lawrence county), 16 inches in Tupper Lake (Franklin county), 15 inches in Lake Placid (Essex county) and 14 inches in Saranac Lake (Franklin county).||Significant accumulations of heavy wet snowfall accounted for scattered tree damage and power outages as well as numerous vehicle accidents across the region.

On Monday afternoon and evening, October 28th, 2008, a frontal system stalled across the eastern seaboard, with an area of light rain across eastern New York State. A wave of low pressure began to organize along this front just off the New Jersey coast early Tuesday morning. This low pressure system rapidly intensified into a major early season winter storm as it crawled up the Hudson River Valley Tuesday and Tuesday evening. As a result, a widespread area of heavy precipitation spread across most of central and eastern New York by Tuesday morning.  Northerly winds developed, which drew down colder air and resulted in a changeover to snow late Monday night over the highest elevations, with snow levels dropping to around 1500 feet in upstate New York and to around 1000 feet in the Catskills by late Tuesday morning.  ||Locations above 1500 feet in the Catskills received 1 to 2 feet of heavy wet snow. Roxbury, NY at 2500 feet received 25.6 inches of snow. Across elevations between 1000 and 1500 feet in the Catskills, snowfall ranged from 2 to 6 inches. In central New York, above 1500 feet, snowfall ranged from 2 to 8 inches, with up to a foot of snow in Oneida and Madison counties. ||Very strong northwest winds developed across central New York behind this storm. Winds gusts ranged from 40 to 50 mph in most places. In areas where there was heavy wet snow, major damage was done to trees and power lines leading to widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 7 inches in Cazenovia to 15.5 inches in Morrisville, at an elevation of 1600 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong cold frontal passage, and the extratropical remnants of Ike led to strong winds across the southern and western Adirondacks, the western and central Mohawk Valley, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and Schoharie Valley of eastern New York during the early morning hours of Monday September 15th. Measured wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were recorded, leading to numerous downed trees, tree limbs and power lines across the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, large tree limbs and power lines were reported down in and near Russia due to strong winds.

Measured wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were recorded, leading to numerous downed trees, tree limbs and power lines across the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, large tree limbs and power lines were reported down due to strong winds. Areas that were particularly affected included Fairfield, Dolgeville, and Newport.||In addition, a trained spotter reported a measured wind gust to 50 mph in Fairfield at 0305 LST.
