EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, large tree limbs and power lines were reported down due to strong winds. Areas that were most affected included Lake Pleasant and Indian Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low center that was the remnants of Hurricane Ike reached the lower Great Lakes region during the late evening of the 14th and brought high winds to the area as it lifted from Indiana across southern Ontario. Wind gusts were measured to 66 mph. The winds downed trees and power lines throughout the area. The wet soil conditions contributed to the number of downed trees. Debris blocked many streets and highways. There were numerous reports of homes, building and automobiles being damaged by fallen trees. In Seneca Point, winds ripped the roof off a barn. Several school districts were forced to cancel classes on the 15th out of safety for the students. In Adams Center, about half of the pool roof at Clarke Senior High School blew off. A bumper crop of late season corn was damaged. It was estimated in Jefferson and Lewis Counties that nearly 70% of the crop was flattened. Marine interests were not spared the wrath of the storm. In Buffalo, three floating docks were ripped from their concrete footings and tilted making them inaccessible to several hundred slip holders. Two boats were capsized off Buffalo. In Chaumont Bay, Jefferson County, several boats were grounded when a dock broke adrift. The storm surge was estimated between 10 and 12 feet at the east end of Lake Ontario.  In Oswego Harbor, two sailboats were sunk. Damages were estimated due to the widespread nature of the event. Specific measured wind gusts included: 66 m.p.h. at Oswego, 59 m.p.h. at Niagara Falls and Attica, 55 m.p.h. at Pembroke and Victor, 54 m.p.h. at Albion, 53 m.p.h. at Kendall, Brockport, Geneseo, and Canandaigua.

A strong cold front swept from west to east Saturday evening, preceded and accompanied by showers and isolated thunderstorms, some of which contained strong wind gusts and a brief period of very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down near Salem due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept from west to east Saturday evening, preceded and accompanied by showers and isolated thunderstorms, some of which contained strong wind gusts and a brief period of very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of heavy rain led to significant ponding of water on a road in Stillwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna impacted Southeast New York, making landfall near the Nassau/Suffolk County border around 10:35 pm on the 6th.  Storm total rainfall ranged from 1.66 inches at Port Jervis to 5.92 inches at New City.  The highest sustained wind of 33 knots (38 mph) with a peak gust of 45 knots (52 mph) was recorded at Shinnecock Inlet.  The lowest sea-level pressure was 996 mb. at Shirley/Brookhaven Airport, Westhampton Beach, and Montauk Airport.  Coastal storm tides of 2 feet or less above astronomical tide levels were common, with only minor|beach erosion reported.  Near the coast, as well as inland, only scattered trees were reported down due to the wind, except along the south shore of Long Island where numerous trees were downed, causing minor property damage.  No fatalities or injuries were attributed to the winds.  All of the associated effects of Hanna resulted in estimated property damage of just under $70,000 and a minimal amount of crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees were reported down in Manhattan from Tropical Storm Hanna. Wind gusts ranged from 24 kt. (28 mph) at Central Park to 38 kt. (44 mph) reported at Public School 124. Rainfall of 3.54 inches was measured in Central Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna impacted Southeast New York, making landfall near the Nassau/Suffolk County border around 10:35 pm on the 6th. Storm total rainfall ranged from 1.66 inches at Port Jervis to 5.92 inches at New City. Periods of torrential rain from heavy showers and thunderstorms caused flash flooding in many locations, which included urban, small stream and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warburton Ave. near Arthur St. in Yonkers was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna impacted Southeast New York, making landfall near the Nassau/Suffolk County border around 10:35 pm on the 6th.  Storm total rainfall ranged from 1.66 inches at Port Jervis to 5.92 inches at New City.  The highest sustained wind of 33 knots (38 mph) with a peak gust of 45 knots (52 mph) was recorded at Shinnecock Inlet.  The lowest sea-level pressure was 996 mb. at Shirley/Brookhaven Airport, Westhampton Beach, and Montauk Airport.  Coastal storm tides of 2 feet or less above astronomical tide levels were common, with only minor|beach erosion reported.  Near the coast, as well as inland, only scattered trees were reported down due to the wind, except along the south shore of Long Island where numerous trees were downed, causing minor property damage.  No fatalities or injuries were attributed to the winds.  All of the associated effects of Hanna resulted in estimated property damage of just under $70,000 and a minimal amount of crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees were reported down in Northeastern Suffolk County from Tropical Storm Hanna. Rainfall reports ranged from 3.04 inches at Mattituck to 3.09 inches in Upton (Brookhaven National Laboratory).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep upper level closed low and associated pool of cold air aloft dropped south through the lower Great Lakes. Thunderstorms developed across the eastern Finger Lakes and eastern Lake Ontario Region during the afternoon hours. Hail, up to an inch-and-a-quarter diameter, fell in Cayuga, Ontario and Oswego counties. Just outside of Victor, Ontario County, the large hail shattered automobile windshields. Lightning from the thunderstorms struck the Bristol Springs Free Church bell tower.
An upper level cold pool provided sufficient instability to produce isolated severe thunderstorms in Nassau and Suffolk counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted at the tail end of a thunderstorm over Long Island Sound about 3 miles North of Port Jefferson Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area on June 14, 2008 which produced widespread flash flooding across portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occupied vehicles were stuck in rising water at the intersection of Queens Blvd. and the Van Wyck Expressway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very energetic mid-atmospheric disturbance moved across the Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of June 10th. This developed a surface low along a cold front, which moved across northern New York during the afternoon. These features moved across a very warm, humid and unstable airmass draped across New York that resulted in two rounds of widespread severe thunderstorms.||The first round moved across northern New York during the early afternoon hours and the second round occurred during the early evening.||In northern New York (Clinton, Essex, Franklin and St. Lawrence counties)...hundreds to thousands of trees were damaged, downed or uprooted which caused downed power lines, fires and structural damage to buildings and vehicles. Tens of thousands of customers lost power due to the storms, with some outages that lasted several days. Some communities affected included Potsdam, Parishville and Colton (St. Lawrence county), Moira, Malone and Vermontville (Franklin county), Peru, Plattsburgh and Ellenburg (Clinton county) as well as Wilmington, Westport and Jay (Essex county).||Several fallen trees resulted in injuries and one death resulted from a motorcyclist driving into a downed tree across the road in Wilmington (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines down across Port Douglas Road and throughout Keeseville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance, coupled with a passing trough of low pressure at the surface, produced several severe thunderstorms on Long Island, especially in Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail was reported at Round Swamp Road near Exit 39 on the Northern State Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front that crossed the Tri-State on the 15th, produced some severe thunderstorms across portions of the New York City Metro Area, Long Island, and the Lower Hudson Valley, including a microburst in Southern Westchester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst impacted the city of Mount Vernon, bringing down large trees, with the most significant damage on Packman Avenue. Based upon the survey, winds with the microburst were estimated from 65 to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over central Lake Erie and moved onshore into Chautauqua county. The storms produced three-quarter inch hail in Westfield and downed trees and branches in Barcelona. Scattered power outages were reported.
Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across central New York in a hot and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front. Many of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trampoline and swing set were blown away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the upper atmosphere brought showers and scattered thunderstorms to the area. Isolated storms produced hail and strong winds.

A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomolous slow moving upper level low pressure system in combination with a weak surface cold front and local seabreezes triggered thunderstorms producing torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding on Long Island and in portions of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New York Ave. was closed in Halesite due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system over the Gulf Coast Friday morning March 7th, interacted with a frontal boundary approaching from the Ohio Valley as it moved northeast. The frontal boundary became nearly stationary across the coastal plain Friday night and a wave of low pressure developed along it. This low passed over the region during the overnight period of March 7th to March 8th followed by the main area of low pressure on Saturday, March 8th, with rainfall totals from both systems between 1 1/2 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bronx River Parkway was closed in both directions at Cross County Parkway in Mount Vernon and the Saw Mill River Parkway was closed southbound at Grant Street due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms accompanied the passage of a cold front across parts of the Finger Lakes and the eastern Lake Ontario regions. Hail up to one inch in diameter was reported in Irondequoit, Rochester, Brighton and Walworth. The downburst winds from the thunderstorms downed trees and power lines in Oswego and Adams. Downpours that accompanied the storms caused brief urban flooding in the Rochester metro area.

A developing upper level trough, combined with several weak surface boundaries, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms during Sunday afternoon and evening on June 22nd, some of which became severe.||Additional isolated severe thunderstorms developed late Sunday night, into the early morning hours of Monday June 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Schenectady during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an upper level trough, and weak cold front produced isolated to scattered thunderstorms during Tuesday afternoon on June 24th. Large hail accompanied some of these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Raymertown during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level dynamics, well ahead of a cold front approaching from the west, triggered widespread showers and thunderstorms across the southern and western Adirondacks, the Mohawk Valley, the Lake George and Saratoga region, the upper Hudson Valley, and portions of the Capital Region during the late afternoon hours of Saturday July 26th, into the early morning hours of Sunday July 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Rosendale Road in Niskayuna during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in an unstable airmass ahead of an approaching cold front. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds the downed power lines in Niagara Falls.  The strong thunderstorms dropped several inches of rain across parts of Wyoming County that resulted in flash flooding from Gainesville to Silver Springs.
A cold front moved across the area on June 14, 2008 which produced widespread flash flooding across portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bronx River Parkway was closed in both directions between Harney Road and the Sprain Brook Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure at the surface coupled with a passing upper level disturbance triggered a round of Severe Thunderstorms producing penny to quarter size hail across the Lower Hudson Valley, Long Island, and parts of New York City on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system spawned numerous severe thunderstorms over the Lower Hudson Valley, New York City Metro, and Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching cold front from the west, and an unseasonably hot, humid and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during Tuesday afternoon and evening across eastern New York and adjacent western New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were reported down near Catskill due to strong thunderstorm winds. In addition, a large tree reportedly fell on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area producing thunderstorms with torrential downpours which caused isolated flash flooding in Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A school bus became stuck in high waters (up to the bumper) at the intersection of Creek Rd. and New York Ave. in Halesite. At least 4 children were rescued by the local fire department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak low pressure system moving east out of eastern Ontario. Some of the storms reached severe levels, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 55 mph wind gust was also estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary retreated to the north of the region during Friday, July 18th, allowing hot and humid conditions to overspread eastern New York and adjacent western New England. This contributed to the development of numerous thunderstorms during Friday afternoon and evening, some of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down in Central Bridge due to strong thunderstorm winds. This fallen tree closed Grovenors Corners Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system along a nearly stationary boundary intensified as it tracked northeast from western Pennsylvania into central New York from Wednesday morning on July 23rd, through Thursday morning on July 24th. A cold front associated with this low slowly moved east across eastern New York late Wednesday afternoon into early Thursday morning.||The combination of strong upper level dynamics, and abundant moisture in place, led to widespread showers and thunderstorms containing heavy rainfall. This led to flooding across portions of eastern New York from Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in numerous road closures across Schenectady County during the late afternoon and evening hours.||Several roads were also washed out, including, but not limited to, State Route 160 and culverts near the New York State Thruway overpass in Pattersonville, Crawford Road, North Kelly Road, and the bridge at Kelly Station Road and Route 7.||Additional damage occurred to a section of Route 5S near Rotterdam Junction.||In addition, Interstate 90, the New York State Thruway, was closed between exits 26 and 27 due to flooding, with several vehicles disabled in the flood waters.||Other roads that were closed included, but were not limited to, Guilderland Road at Cleveland Road, Route 147 between Charlton Road and West Glenville Road, Junction Road, Creek Road and Shun Pike Road near Esperance.||High water also scoured out a railroad embankment and the tracks that a train was on, stranding the train until the track could be repaired.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system over the Gulf Coast Friday morning March 7th, interacted with a frontal boundary approaching from the Ohio Valley as it moved northeast. The frontal boundary became nearly stationary across the coastal plain Friday night and a wave of low pressure developed along it. This low passed over the region during the overnight period of March 7th to March 8th followed by the main area of low pressure on Saturday, March 8th, with rainfall totals from both systems between 1 1/2 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 17 near Albany Post Rd. was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours ahead of an approaching cold front. Hail up to one inch in diameter was reported in Wolcott, Wayne county. The damaging winds produced by the storms downed trees and power lines in Williamson, Wayne county and Randolph, Cattaraugus county, with scattered power outages reported.
Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed as a weak short wave moved across the region.

A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.

A low pressure system over southern Ontario pulled a cold front across the area during the afternoon and evening. Widespread thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with many of the storms producing large hail. The hail storms caused significant damage to fruit crops across much of upstate New York, including apples, peaches, strawberries and cherries. Some growers reported losing as much as 75 percent of their crops, which also damaged corn and other vegetable crops.

A cold front moved east from the Great Lakes, through upstate New York during the day. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of and along the front, and some of the storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts.
A weak surface trough produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. A few storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds across the Finger Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell on houses, and trees and wires were blown down in Genoa and Locke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area producing thunderstorms with torrential downpours which caused isolated flash flooding in Southeastern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 20 inches of standing water was reported on Fox Island Rd. in Port Chester. Basements of residences on Midland Ave. in Rye were also flooded with water. The ASOS at Westchester County Airport in White Plains recorded 1.39 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley on Tuesday March 4th, into south central New York State by Wednesday morning on March 5th.||Abundant moisture was associated with this system, which led to widespread heavy rainfall across much of east central New York and western New England, which began late Tuesday night, and persisted into early Wednesday afternoon.||Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell during this event. This heavy rainfall, in combination with frozen ground and snowmelt led to flooding in many areas within the Capital Region, as well as within the Lake George and Saratoga region, the Schoharie Valley, the eastern Catskills and mid Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Springtown Road was closed between Route 299 and Dug Road due to flooding.||In addition, Route 299 was closed from the Wallkill River Bridge to Libertyville Road due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance overriding a ridge over the Northeast United States produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across upstate New York. Several of the storms reached severe levels, with damaging winds and large hail.

A strong cold front moved through a moderately unstable air mass across northern New York during the late afternoon and early evening hours of August 18th. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms accompanied this cold front, especially in St. Lawrence county with several reports of damaging winds. Other damaging wind reports associated with these storms occurred in Franklin and Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down across power lines along Route 30 near Duane Center along with several large tree limbs in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front extending from southern New England through the Delmarva region pulled eastward as low pressure developed along the boundary. As moisture pulled northward, heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed, producing severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front extending from southern New England through the Delmarva region pulled eastward as low pressure developed along the boundary. As moisture pulled northward, heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed, producing severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flooding Route 7 by Dives Crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved from west to east across eastern New York and western New England during Monday afternoon on June 16th. Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed with and preceding the front, many of which were accompanied by large hail.||The widespread and large hail led to significant damage of tree fruits, berries and vegetables across eastern New York, with preliminary damage estimates exceeding $23 million. The worst hit area was within the mid Hudson Valley region, particularly across portions of Ulster and Columbia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Esperance during a thunderstorm.
